questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q:
The variation of the rate of an enzyme catalyzed reaction with substrate concentration is correctly represented by graph

(a)
 

(b)
 

(c)
 

(d)
questionIds.solution:
The substrate concentration influences enzyme activity. As the substrate concentration increases the rate of reaction also increases. This is because the more substrate molecules will interact with enzyme molecules, the more products will be formed.

However, after a certain concentration, further increase in substrate concentration will have no effect on the rate of reaction, since the substrate concentration will no longer be the limiting factor. At this stage, enzyme molecules become saturated and work at their maximum possible rate.

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: Choose the Incorrect Statement about Dalton's Atomic Theory
options: chemical reactions involve reorganization of atoms
Compounds are formed when atoms of different elements combine in any ratio.

Compounds are formed when atoms of different elements combine in any ratio. All the atoms of a given element have identical properties including identical 
mass.
questionIds.solution: In compound atoms of different elements combine in fixed ratio by mass.

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q:
Two statements in respect of drug-enzyme interaction are given below

Statement I : Action of an enzyme can be blocked only when an inhibitor blocks the active site of the enzyme.

Statement II : An inhibitor can form a strong covalent bond with the enzyme.

In the light of the above statements. Choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
questionIds.solution:
The first function of an enzyme is to hold the substrate for a chemical reaction. Active sites of enzymes hold the substrate molecule in a suitable position, so that it can be attacked by the reagent effectively.

Substrates bind to the active site of the enzyme through a variety of interactions such as ionic bonding, hydrogen bonding, van der Waals interaction or dipole-dipole interaction.

Drugs inhibit activities of enzymes. These can block the binding site of the enzyme and prevent the binding of substrate, or can inhibit the catalytic activity of the enzyme. Such drugs are called enzyme inhibitors

Some drugs do not bind to active sites. These bind to different site of enzyme called allosteric sites.

This binding of inhibitor at allosteric site changes the shape of the active site in such a way that substrate cannot recognise it

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: In the above conversion the correct sequence of reagents to be added is
questionIds.solution: The required product can be obtained by the following method. In this method nitro toluene is subjected to bromination, bromo group is substituted at ortho position to the methyl as it is more activating group. Then nitro group is reduced to amine group with iron metal in acid. The amine group undergoes diazotisation with nitrous acid. The diazonium group is replaced with chloro group in the presence of cuprous chloride. Finally, methyl group is oxidised in the presence of potassium permanganate.

questionIds.q: It has been found that for a chemical reaction with rise in temperature by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> the rate constant gets doubled. Assuming a reaction to be occurring at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>, the value of activation energy is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>kJmol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. [nearest integer] (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ln</mi><mn>10</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo><mi>log</mi><mn>2</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>30</mn></math>)
questionIds.solution:
Arrhenius equation for the rate constant of a reaction in terms of energy of activation at two different temperatures is given by

logK2K1=Ea2.3R1T1-1T2

Rise in temperature =9 K

Initial temperature =300 K

logK309K300=Ea2.3R1300-1309

log2=Ea2.3R9300×309

Ea=2.3×0.30×300×3099

Ea=58.988×103 J=58.988kJ=59kJ

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: For the reaction equilibrium $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_2(\mathrm{~g})$ the concentrations of $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4$ and $\mathrm{NO}_2$ at equilibrium are $4.8 \times 10^{-2}$ and $1.2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$ respectively. The value of $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$ for the reaction is
questionIds.solution: $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]^2}{\left[\mathrm{~N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4\right]}=\frac{\left[1.2 \times 10^{-2}\right]^2}{\left[4.8 \times 10^{-2}\right]}=3 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L}$

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q:
For the reaction;
Al→2Bg
ΔU=2.1kcal,ΔS=20calK-1 at 300K.
Hence ΔG in kcal is _____________.
questionIds.solution:
∆H=∆U+∆ngRT
=2.1×103+22300
=2100+1200
=3300cal
∆G=∆H-T∆S
=3300-30020
=3300-6000
=-2700cals=-2.7kcal

Gibbs free energy can be visualized as the amount of useful energy present in a thermodynamic system that can be utilized to perform some useful work.

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: From the magnetic behaviour of $\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_4\right]^{2-}$ (paramagnetic) and $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4\right]$ (diamagnetic), choose the correct geometry and oxidation state.
questionIds.solution:
$\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_4\right]^{2-}$
$\mathrm{Ni}^{+2}-[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^8 4 \mathrm{~s}^0 \rightarrow \mathrm{sp}^3$, Tetrahedral
Number of unpaired electron $=2$ paramagentic
$\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4\right]$
$\mathrm{Ni}(0) \rightarrow[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{10} 4 \mathrm{~s}^0$ (After rearrangement)
No unpaired electron
$\mathrm{sp}^3$, Tetrahedral, Diamagnetic

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questionIds.q: Which is the correct order of second ionization potential of $\mathrm{C}, \mathrm{N}, \mathrm{O}$ and $\mathrm{F}$ in the following ?
questionIds.solution: The second ionization potential means removal of electron from cation $\mathrm{C}^{+}=1 s^2 2 s^2 2 p^1, \mathrm{~N}^{+}=1 s^2 2 s^2 2 p^2$ $\mathrm{O}^{+}=1 s^2 2 s^2 2 p^3, \mathrm{~F}^{+}=1 s^2 2 s^2 2 p^4$ Therefore $\mathrm{O}>\mathrm{F}>\mathrm{N}>\mathrm{C}$

questionIds.q: The energy of an electron in first Bohr's orbit of H atom is <math> <mrow> <mo>−</mo><mn>13.6</mn><mtext> </mtext><mtext>eV</mtext><mtext>.</mtext></mrow> </math> The energy value of electron in the first excited state of <math> <mrow> <msup> <mrow> <mtext>Li</mtext></mrow> <mrow> <mtext>2+</mtext></mrow> </msup> </mrow> </math> is :
questionIds.solution:
For H atom & H like species
The electronic energy in the nth orbit is

E n = - 13.6 z 2 n 2 eV

E n Li 2 + = - 13.6 × 9 n 2 eV = - 122.4 eV n 2

If n = 2

E 2 Li 2 + = - 122.4 2 2 = - 30.6 eV

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : An allotrope of oxygen is an important intermediate in the formation of reducing smog.

Statement II : Gases such as oxides of nitrogen and sulphur present in troposphere contribute to the formation of photochemical smog. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
questionIds.solution:
Both Statement I and Statement II are false

Reducing smog is a mixture of smoke, fog and sulphur dioxide. Tropospheric pollutants such as hydrocarbon and nitrogen oxide contribute to the formation of photochemical smog.

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q:
Gibbs energy vs T plot for the formation of oxides is given below.

For the given diagram, the correct statement is-
questionIds.solution:
Ellingham diagrams are a particular graphical form of the principle that the thermodynamic feasibility of a reaction depends on the sign of ΔG, the Gibbs free energy change, which is equal to ΔH − TΔS, where ΔH is the enthalpy change and ΔS is the entropy change. Hence, element below in Ellingham diagram can reduce oxide of element above it.

C can reduce FeO at 600 ºC.

=80×0.4×100188×0.5

=34%

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: In which one of the following sets all species show disproportionation reaction?
questionIds.solution:
Disproportionation reactions are a special type of redox reactions. One of the reactants in a disproportionation reaction always contains an element that can exist in at least three oxidation states. The element of reacting species is in intermediate oxidation state and simultaneously gets oxidised and reduced. MnO42-,ClO2-,Cl2 and Mn3+ are having intermediate oxidation states.

Note: No option is correct in the actual question. One option is modified for its correctness.

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: A hydroxy compound $(\mathrm{X})$ with molar mass $122 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ is acetylated with acetic anhydride, using a large excess of the reagent ensuring complete acetylation of all hydroxyl groups. The product obtained has a molar mass of $290 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. The number of hydroxyl groups present in compound (X) is:
questionIds.solution:
In acetylation, each hydroxyl group reacts:
R-OH + (CH₃CO)₂O → R-O-CO-CH₃ + CH₃COOH.
Each -OH is converted to -OCOCH₃, resulting in a net mass increase of 42 g/mol per hydroxyl group (addition of acetyl group mass 43, removal of hydrogen mass 1).
Given: Original molar mass = 122 g/mol, Product molar mass = 290 g/mol
Total mass increase = 290 - 122 = 168 g/mol
Number of hydroxyl groups = 168 ÷ 42 = 4

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The sum, of the coefficients of the first <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> terms in the binomial expansion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>100</mn></msup><mo>,</mo></math> is equal to
questionIds.solution:
We know that 1-x100=C0100-C1100·x+C2100·x2-.......-C49100·x49+.......+C100100·x100

Now let, the sum of first fifty coefficients be S.

⇒S=C0100-C1100+C2100-.......-C49100

Let us substitute x=1 in the expansion.

⇒1-1100=C0100-C1100·1+C2100·12-.......-C49100·149+.......+C100100·1100

⇒0=C0100-C1100+C2100-.......-C49100+C50100-C51100+C52100.......+C100100

There are total of 101 terms and the middle term is T51=C50100.

As Crn=Cn-rn

⇒0=C0100-C1100+C2100-.......-C49100+C50100-C49100+C48100.......+C0100

⇒0=C50100+C0100-C1100+C2100-.......-C49100-C49100+C48100.......+C0100

⇒0=C50100+2C0100-C1100+C2100-.......-C49100

⇒-C501002=C0100-C1100+C2100-.......-C49100

⇒C0100-C1100+C2100-.......-C49100=-C501002

Therefore, the required answer is -C501002=-12×10050C4999=-C4999.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics

questionIds.solution:
Given ATA=3I3

02x2x2yy-y1-1102y12xy-12x-y1=300030003

8x20006y20003=300030003

On comparing the two matrices, we get

8x2=3 and 6y2=3

⇒x2=38 and y2=12

⇒x=±38 and y=±12

For each of x & y we have two possible values, hence there are total 2×2=4 combinations of values are possible.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The set of all $\alpha$, for which the vectors $\vec{a}=\alpha t \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}$ and $\vec{b}=t \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-2 \alpha t \hat{k}$ are inclined at an obtuse angle for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$, is
questionIds.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=\alpha \hat{\mathrm{i}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=\mathrm{t} \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \alpha \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \text { so } \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}} < 0, \forall \mathrm{t} \in \mathrm{R} \\ & \alpha \mathrm{t}^2-12+6 \alpha \mathrm{t} < 0 \\ & \alpha \mathrm{t}^2+6 \alpha \mathrm{t}-12 < 0, \forall \mathrm{t} \in \mathrm{R} \\ & \alpha < 0 \text {, and } \mathrm{D} < 0 \\ & 36 \alpha^2+48 \alpha < 0 \\ & 12 \alpha(3 \alpha+4) < 0 \\ & \frac{-4}{3} < \alpha < 0\end{aligned}$
also for $\mathrm{a}=0, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}} < 0$
hence a $\alpha \in\left(\frac{-4}{3}, 0\right]$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The equations of sides <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> of a triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>λ</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>λ</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>λ</mi></mrow></mfenced><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>λ</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> respectively. Its vertex <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo></math>axis and its orthocentre is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. The length of the tangent from the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> to the part of the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi></math> in the first quadrant is
questionIds.solution:
Given,

The equations of sides AB and AC of a triangle ABC and have orthocentre at 1,2

So, AB≡λ+1x+λy=4 ........1

And AC≡λx+1-λy+λ=0 ......2 

Also given vertex A is on the y-axis

So, putting the value x=0 in equation 1 & 2 we get,

y=4λ & y=λλ-1

⇒4λ=λλ-1

⇒λ=2

Now putting the value of λ in both equation we get,

AB≡3x+2y=4 ........3

AC≡2x-y+2=0 ......4

Now solving above equation we get, A0,2

Now let Cα,2α+2 {using equation 4}

Now we know that slopes of perpendicular lines is -1,

So, from diagram slope of altitude through C×slope of AB we get,

2αα-1×-32=-1

⇒α=-12

Hence, point C will be C-12,1

Now let the equation of tangent be y=mx+32m which passes through point C-12,1

So, 1=m-12+32m

⇒m2+2m-3=0

⇒m=1, -3

Also we know that point of contact of tangent to parabola is given by am2,2am≡32,3

Hence, CT=32+122+3-12=8=22

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let the function fx=loge1+5x-loge1+αxxif x≠010if x=0 be continuous at x=0. Then α is equal to
questionIds.solution:
fx=ln1+5x-ln1+αxx x≠010 x=0

For fx to be continuous

 limx→0fx=f0

limx→0ln1+5x-ln1+αxx=10

Using expansion of ln1+x=x-x22+x33- ...., we get

limx→05x-25x22+……-αx-α2x22+……x=10

i.e. 5-α=10⇒α=-5

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>…</mo></math>  be an increasing geometric progression of positive real numbers. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>A</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></msub><msub><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>A</mi></mrow><mn>5</mn></msub><msub><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>A</mi></mrow><mn>7</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>1296</mn></mfrac></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>7</mn><mn>36</mn></mfrac></math>, then, the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>6</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>8</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>10</mn></msub></math> is equal to
questionIds.solution:
Given 

A1A3A5A7=11296  

Expanding using An=A1rn-1 formula we get,

 ⇒A1A1r2A1r4A1r6=11296

⇒A14r12=11296⇒A1r3=16     ...i

Also given ,

A2+A4=736⇒A1r+A1r3=736

⇒A1r+16=736

⇒A1r=136   ...ii

Dividing equation (i) from equation (ii) we get, r2=6 

Now A6+A8+A10=A1r5+A1r7+A1r9

=A1r3r2+r4+r6 =166+36+216  =43

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>p</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> be the parabola passing through the points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. If the area of the region <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="{" close="}"><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>:</mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>≤</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi><mo>≤</mo><mi>p</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mfenced><mrow><mi>π</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>A</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to ________ .
questionIds.solution:
Let, the parabola be y=ax2+bx+c,

Now given parabola is passing through -1,0, 0,1 & 1,0

Now putting the value in the above equation of parabola we get,

a-b+c=0 ......1, c=1  ....2 and  a+b+c=0 ....3

Now solving above three equations we get,

Parabola as y=1-x2

Now area of region between y=1-x2 and x+12+y-12≤1 is given by,

Now from above diagram, required area will be,

A=∫-10-x2+1-x+12dx

⇒A=π4-13

Hence, 12(π-4A)=16

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mo>∫</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></msubsup><mfrac><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>sin</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mi>x</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mi>cos</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfrac><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi></math> is equal to
questionIds.solution:
Let, I=∫π3π22+3sinxsinx1+cosxdx

Now rearranging the above integral we get,

I=∫π3π22sinx1+cosxdx+∫π3π231+cosxdx

⇒I=∫π3π22sinxsin2x1+cosxdx+∫π3π232cos2x2dx

⇒I=∫π3π22sinxsin2x1+cosxdx⏟I1+32∫π3π2sec2x2dx⏟I2

⇒I=∫π3π22sinxsin2x1+cosxdx⏟I1+32×2tanx2π3π2⏟I2

⇒I=∫π3π22sinxsin2x1+cosxdx⏟I1+31-13

Now solving I1 we get,

I1=∫π3π22sinxsin2x1+cosxdx

⇒I1=2∫π3π21sinx1+cosxdx

⇒I1=2∫π3π212tanx21+tan2x21+1-tanx21+tan2x2dx

⇒I1=12∫π3π21+tan2x2sec2x2tanx2dx

Put tanx2=v⇒12sec2x2dx=dv

⇒I1=∫1311+v2dvv

⇒I1=logev+v22131

⇒I1=loge1-loge13+121-13

⇒I1=loge13+13

So,

I=13+loge3+31-13

⇒I=103-3+loge3

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If the fourth term in the Binomial expansion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow><mn>8</mn></msub><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced><mn>6</mn></msup><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>8</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><mo>,</mo></math> then a value of <math><mi>x</mi></math> is
questionIds.solution:
The general term in the binomial expansion a+bn is Tr+1=Crnan-rbr.

Given, the fourth term in the expansion of 2x+xlog8x6 is 

T4=20×87

⇒C362x3xlog8x3=20×87

⇒6!3!·3!2x3xlog8x3=20×237

⇒6×5×4×3!3×2×1·3!2x3xlog8x3=20×221

⇒202x3xlog8x3=20×221

⇒2xxlog8x3=273

⇒2xxlog8x=27

⇒1xxlog8x=26

⇒xlog8x-1=82

Taking log both side to the base 8

⇒log8xlog8x-1=log882

Using logamn=nlogam, we get

⇒(log8x-1)log8x=2

⇒t-1t=2                (Let log8x=t)

⇒t2-t-2=0

⇒t=2 or -1

⇒log8x=2 or -1

If logax=b, ⇒x=ab,

⇒x=82 or 8-1

⇒x=64  or 18.

questionIds.q: For <math><mi>a</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> let the curves <math><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>:</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mi>x</mi></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>:</mo><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mi>y</mi></math> intersect at origin <math><mi>O</mi></math> and a point <math><mi>P</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Let the line <math><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>b</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo><</mo><mi>b</mi><mo><</mo><mi>a</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> intersect the chord <math><mi>O</mi><mi>P</mi></math> and the <math><mi>x</mi></math> -axis at points <math><mi>Q</mi></math> and <math><mi>R</mi><mo>,</mo></math> respectively. If the line <math><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>b</mi></math> bisects the area bounded by the curves, <math><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo></math> and the area of <math><mo>∆</mo><mi>O</mi><mi>Q</mi><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> then ‘ <math><mi>a</mi></math> ’ satisfies the equation:
questionIds.solution:
∫0bax-x2adx=a26

⇒ 23ab32-b33a=a26           ...1

Also area of ΔOQR=12 

12b2=12⇒b=1

Put in 1

⇒ 4aa-2=a3

⇒ a6+4a3+4=16a3

⇒ a6-12a3+4=0

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If $p$ and $q$ are the roots of the equation $x^2+p x+q=0$, then
questionIds.solution:
$p+q=-p$ and $p q=q \Rightarrow q(p-1)=0 \Rightarrow q=0$ or $p=1$
If $q=0$, then $p=0$. i.e. $p=q \quad \therefore p=1$ and $q=-2$

questionIds.q: The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>8</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo>.</mo><mi>cos</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>8</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>8</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo>.</mo><mi>sin</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>8</mn></mfrac></mfenced></math> is:
questionIds.solution:
⇒cos3π8cosπ2-π8+sin3π8sinπ2-π8

⇒cos3π8sinπ8+sin3π8cosπ8

⇒sinπ8cosπ8sin2π8+cos2π8

⇒12sinπ4

⇒122

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The value of the integral $\int_{\frac{\pi}{24}}^{\frac{5 \pi}{24}} \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{1+\sqrt[3]{\tan 2 x}}$ is :
questionIds.solution:
Let $I = \int_{\pi/24}^{5\pi/24} \frac{dx}{1+\sqrt[3]{\tan 2x}}$.
Apply King's rule ($x \to \pi/4 - x$):
$I = \int_{\pi/24}^{5\pi/24} \frac{dx}{1+\sqrt[3]{\cot 2x}} = \int_{\pi/24}^{5\pi/24} \frac{\sqrt[3]{\tan 2x}}{1+\sqrt[3]{\tan 2x}}dx$.
Adding: $2I = \int_{\pi/24}^{5\pi/24} dx = \frac{5\pi}{24} - \frac{\pi}{24} = \frac{\pi}{6}$.
$I = \frac{\pi}{12}$.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>{</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>ℝ</mi><mo>:</mo><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>≤</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>}</mo><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="{" close="}"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>ℝ</mi><mo>:</mo><msup><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></msup><msup><mfenced><mrow><munderover><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mi>r</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mo>∞</mo></munderover><mfrac><mn>3</mn><msup><mn>10</mn><mi>r</mi></msup></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo><</mo><msup><mn>3</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>[</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>]</mo></math> denotes greatest integer function. Then,
questionIds.solution:
Given,

A={x∈ℝ:x+3+x+4≤3} & B=x∈ℝ:3x∑r=1∞310rx-3<3-3x,

Now solving A we get,

x+3+x+4≤3

⇒x+3+x+4≤3

⇒2x≤-4

⇒x≤-2

⇒x<-1⇒A=-∞,-1

Now solving B we get,

3x∑r=1∞310rx-3<3-3x

Now using infinite G.P formula S∞=a1-r we get,

⇒3x3·1101-110x-3<3-3x

⇒3x13x-3<3-3x

⇒3x-x+3+3x<1

⇒33x+1<1

⇒3x+1<0

⇒x<-1⇒B=-∞,-1

⇒A=B

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>a</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mi>α</mi><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>b</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mi>α</mi><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi mathvariant="bold-italic">R</mi></math>. If the area of the parallelogram whose adjacent sides are represented by the vectors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>a</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>b</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>15</mn><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>α</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced></msqrt></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><msup><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mover accent="true"><mi>a</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mfenced><mrow><mover accent="true"><mi>a</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>·</mo><mover accent="true"><mi>b</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><msup><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mover accent="true"><mi>b</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to
questionIds.solution:
Given, 

a→=αi^+2j^-k^,b→=-2i^+αj^+k^,

Area of parallelogram =a^×b^

a^×b^=α+22+α-22+α2+42

Given a^×b^=15α2+4

So, 2α2+4+α2+42=15α2+4

⇒α2+42=13α2+4

⇒α2+4=13  ∴ α2=9

Now, 2a→2+a→·b→b→2

a→2=α2+4+1=α2+5

|b→|2=4+α2+1=α2+5

a→·b→=-2α+2α-1=-1

∴ 2a→2+a→·b→b→2

2α2+5-1α2+5=α2+5=14

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: In a class of 100 students there are 70 boys whose average marks in a subject are 75. If the average marks of the complete class is 72, then what is the average of the girls?
questionIds.solution:
Total student $=100 ;$ for 70 stds $75 \times 70=5250 \Rightarrow 7200-5250=1950$
$$
\text { Average of girls }=\frac{1950}{30}=65
$$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If both the roots of the quadratic equation $x^2-2 k x+k^2+k-5=0$ are less than 5 , then $\mathrm{k}$ lies in the interval
questionIds.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{-b}{2 a} < 5 \\
& f(5)>0 \\
& \Rightarrow k \in(-\infty, 4) .
\end{aligned}
$$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="{" close="" separators="|"><mtable><mtr><mtd><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow><mi>x</mi></mfrac></mtd><mtd><mo>;</mo><mi>x</mi><mo><</mo><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi>b</mi></mtd><mtd><mo>;</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mfrac><mrow><msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></mfrac></mtd><mtd><mo>;</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced></math> is continuous at <math><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> , then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>b</mi></math> is equal to:
questionIds.solution:
f0-=limx→0-sinaxcos2xx+sin2xcosaxx+sinxx

=limx→0-acos2x+2cosax+1

=a+3

Also, f0=b
f0+=limx→0x13x131+3x13-1x , this is 00form

Apply LH Rule 

⇒f0+=1

∴ a=-2

b=1

∴a+2b=0

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>:</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>∞</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>→</mo><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><munderover><mo>∫</mo><mn>0</mn><mi>x</mi></munderover><mi>t</mi><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>t</mi></mfenced><mi>d</mi><mi>t</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi><mfenced><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>4</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>5</mn></msup><mo>,</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><munderover><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mi>r</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mn>12</mn></munderover><mi>f</mi><mfenced><msup><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfenced></math> is equal to:
questionIds.solution:
Given: 

Fx=∫0xt⋅ftdt

Now, applying Newton Leibnitz Theorem we get,

F'x=x⋅fx

 And Fx2=x4+x5

Let x2=t

⇒Ft=t2+t52

⇒F't=2t+52t32 as F'x=x⋅fx

⇒t⋅ft=2t+52t32

⇒ft=2+52t12

⇒∑r=112fr2=∑r=1122+52r

⇒∑r=112fr2=24+5212132

⇒∑r=112fr2=219

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> is the solution of the differential equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>e</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><msup><mi>e</mi><mi>x</mi></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mn>0</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mfenced><mrow><mi>y</mi><mo>'</mo><mfenced><mn>0</mn></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>log</mi><mi mathvariant="italic">c</mi></msub><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to:
questionIds.solution:
Given,

1+e2xdydx+21+y2ex=0

⇒dy1+y2+2ex1+e2xdx=0 .........(i)

Now integrating both side we get,

⇒∫dy1+y2+∫2ex1+e2xdx=∫0

⇒tan-1y+2tan-1ex=c

∵y0=0

so, C=π2⇒tan-1 y+2 tan-1ex=π2 ....(ii)

Now from equation (i), we get dydxx=0=-1

From equation (ii) we get, y (In 3)=-13

So, 6y'0+(y In 3)2=6-1+13=-4.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi>α</mi></mrow><msqrt><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mn>2</mn><mi>α</mi></msqrt></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>7</mn></mfrac></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mn>2</mn><mi>β</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></mfrac></msqrt><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mn>10</mn></msqrt></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mtext> </mtext><mi>β</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>, then <math><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>, is equal to
questionIds.solution:
Given,

2sinα1+cos2α=17 and 1-cos2β2=110, α, β∈0,π2

Now using cos2θ=2cos2θ-1=1-2sin2θ, we can write

2sin⁡α2cos⁡α=17 and 2sin⁡β2=110


tan⁡α=17 and sin⁡β=110 or tan⁡β=13
tan2β=2tan⁡β1-tan2⁡β=2.131-19=34
tan⁡α+2β=tanα+tan2β1-tanαtan⁡2β=17+341-17·34=4+21282528=1

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><munderover><mo>∫</mo><mn>0</mn><mn>1</mn></munderover><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>x</mi><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><munderover><mo>∫</mo><mn>0</mn><mn>1</mn></munderover><msup><mi>cot</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><munderover><mo>∫</mo><mn>0</mn><mn>1</mn></munderover><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi></math> is equal to
questionIds.solution:
From the given equation

2∫01tan-1⁡xdx= ∫01π2-tan-1⁡1-x+x2dx

⇒2∫01tan-1⁡xdx= ∫01π2 dx- ∫01tan-1⁡1-x+x2dx
 ⇒∫01tan-1⁡1-x+x2dx=π2-2 ∫01tan-1⁡xdx    …i         
Let I1= ∫01tan-1⁡xdx

=tan-1⁡xx01-∫0111+x2xdx

=π4- ∫01x1+x2dx

=π4-12 ∫012x1+x2dx

=π4-12 ln1+x201

=π4-12ln⁡2

By equation i,

∫01tan-1⁡1-x+x2dx= π2-2π4-12ln2=ln⁡2

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let λ∈ℤ, a→=λi^+j^-k^ and b→=3i^-j^+2k^. Let c→ be a vector such that a→+b→+c→×c→=0→, a→·c→=-17 and b→·c→=-20. Then c→×λi^+j^+k^2 is equal to
questionIds.solution:
Given that

a→=λi^+j^-k^

b→=3i^-j^+2k^

Also a→+b→+c→×c→=0→

⇒ka→+b→=c→

a→·c→=-17 and b→·c→=-20

Now, Take a→·c→=-17

⇒kλi^+j^-k^·λi^+j^-k^+3i^-j^+2k^=-17

⇒kλ2+3λ+0-1=-17

 ⇒kλ2+3λ-1=-17 ....(1)

Similarly, on taking b→·c→=-20, we get, 

k3i^-j^+2k^·λi^+j^-k^+3i^-j^+2k^=-20

⇒k3λ+9+2=-20

⇒k3λ+11=-20  ...(2)

Now on solving equation 1 & 2 we get,

⇒20λ2+9λ-207=0

⇒λ=3, -6920

For λ=3, k=-1

⇒c→=-1a→+b→

⇒-λ+3i^+k^=-6i^-k^

⇒c→×λi^+j^+k^=i^j^k^-60-1311=i^-3j^+6k^

⇒c→×λi^+j^+k^2=46

Hence this is the correct option.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The sum of all the elements of the set <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mn>100</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>:</mo><mi>H</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>F</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>24</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is
questionIds.solution:
Given S=1,2,3…100

Now finding the sum of S=100×1012

 Prime factors of 24=23×3

Let nA= Multiples of 2

nB= Multiples of 3

nA∩B= Multiples of 2 & 3

So nA∪B=nA+nB−nA∩B

To have H.C.F to be 1 we need to subtract the sum of multiples of 2 & 3 from sum of set S to get required answer,

So required answer

=100×1012− Sum of nA∪B

=100×1012-2×50×512+332102−162×102 =1633

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>a</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>a</mi><mn>10</mn></msub></math> be an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>P</mi><mo>.</mo></math> with common difference <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>b</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>b</mi><mn>10</mn></msub></math> be a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>G</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>P</mi><mo>.</mo></math> with common ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo></math> Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>c</mi><mi>k</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>a</mi><mi>k</mi></msub><mo>+</mo><msub><mi>b</mi><mi>k</mi></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>k</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>,</mo><mn>10</mn><mo>.</mo></math> If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>c</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>12</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>c</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>13</mn><mo>,</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><munderover><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mn>10</mn></munderover><msub><mi>c</mi><mi>k</mi></msub></math> is equal to ______.
questionIds.solution:
Ck=ak+bk

where ak=a1+(k-1)(-3) and bk=b12k-1

Ck=a1+(k-1)(-3)+b12k-1

∑k=110ck=∑k=110ak+∑k=110bk

C2=a1-3+b1(2)=12   (1)....

C3=a1-6+4 b1=13    (2)......

by solving (1) & (2)
2b1-3=1⇒b1=2 and a1=11

∑k=110ck=10222+9-3+2210-12-1

=-25+2046=2021

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A resistor develops <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math> of thermal energy in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> when a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is passed through it. If the current is increased from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>, what will be the energy developed in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>.
questionIds.solution:
500=1.52×R×20

E=32×R×20

E=2000 J

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A hydraulic automobile lift is designed to lift vehicles of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5000</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>. The area of cross section of the cylinder carrying load is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. The maximum pressure the smaller piston would have to bear is [Assume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
questionIds.solution:
The given data is

m=5000 kgg=10 m s-2A=250×10-4 m2

Using the formula of pressure,

P=FA=mgA
=5000×10250×10-4

=2×106 N m-2=2×106 Pa

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A sphere of radius a and mass m rolls along a horizontal plane with constant speed v0. It encounters an inclined plane at angle θ and climbs upward. Assuming that it rolls without slipping, how far up the sphere will travel?
questionIds.solution:
From energy conservation
mgh=12mv02+12Iω2
⇒mgh=12mv02+12×25ma2×v02a2
⇒gh=12v02+15v02
⇒gh=710v02

From triangle, sin θ=hl
Then h=l sin θ
⇒l sin θ=710v02 g
Thus, the sphere will travel distance, l=710v02 g sin θ.

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q:
Consider following statements:
A. Surface tension arises due to extra energy of the molecules at the interior as compared to the molecules at the surface, of a liquid.
B. As the temperature of liquid rises, the coefficient of viscosity increases.
C. As the temperature of gas increases, the coefficient of viscosity increases
D. The onset of turbulence is determined by Reynold's number.
E. In a steady flow two stream lines never intersect.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
questionIds.solution: Surface tension arises due to extra energy of the molecules at the molecules at the surface as compared at the interior of a liquid. The coefficient of viscosity for a liquid decreases with rise in temperature where as it increases for gases with increase in temperature. The flow is turbulent for a Reynold's number greater than 2000. Stream lines never intersect in a steady flow.

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q:
Diameter of a steel ball is measured using a Vernier calipers which has divisions of 0.1 cm on its main scale (MS) and 10 divisions of its Vernier scale (VS) match 9 divisions on the main scale. Three such measurements for a ball are given as:
S.No. MS (cm) VS divisions
1. 0.5 8
2. 0.5 4
3. 0.5 6

If the zero error is - 0.03 cm, then mean corrected diameter is:
questionIds.solution:
L.C. of Vernier calipers

= 1 main scale divisionTotal divisons vernier scale  

=0.110=0.01 cm

Reading of Vernier calipers

=M.S.R. +L.C×vs divisions.

d1=0.5+0.01×8

d2=0.5+0.01×4

d3=0.5+0.01×6

∴    Measured diameter are respectively

0.58 cm     0.54 cm,    0.56 cm

∴   average diameter = 0.58 + 0.54 + 0.563 

=1.683=0.56

Negative zero error is added in the average diameter to get the corrected diameter

∴   corrected diameter=0.56-(-0.03)

=0.56+0.03=0.59 cm

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: An ideal monatomic gas with pressure $P$, volume $V$ and temperature $T$ is expanded isothermally to a volume $2 V$ and a final pressure $P_i$. If the same gas is expanded adiabatically to a volume $2 \mathrm{~V}$, the final pressure is $P_a$. The ratio $\frac{P_a}{P_i}$ is
questionIds.solution:
For isothermal process :
$$
\begin{aligned}
& P V=P_i 2 V \\
& P=2 P_i
\end{aligned}
$$
For adiabatic process
$$
\mathrm{PV}^\gamma=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{a}}(2 \mathrm{~V})^\gamma
$$
$(\because$ for monatomic gas $\gamma=5 / 3)$
[From (i) ]
or, $\quad 2 \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{V}^{\frac{5}{3}}=P_a(2 \mathrm{~V})^{\frac{5}{3}}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \frac{P_a}{P_i}=\frac{2}{2^{\frac{5}{3}}} \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{P_a}{P_i}=2^{\frac{-2}{3}}
\end{aligned}
$$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: Two capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$ are connected in parallel to a battery. Charge-time graph is shown below for the two capacitors. The energy stored with them are $U_1$ and $U_2$, respectively. Which of the given statements is true?
questionIds.solution:
For a capacitor at steady state
$q=C V \text { and } U=\frac{1}{2} C V^2$

Since $C_1$ and $C_2$ are connected in parallel, $V_1=V_2$.
Also from graph $q_1 < q_2$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \quad C_1 V_1 < C_2 V_2 \\
& \text { i.e. } C_1 < C_2 \text { or } C_2>C_1 \\
& \qquad \frac{U_1}{U_2}=\frac{C_1 V_1^2}{C_2 V_2^2}=\frac{C_1}{C_2} < 1
\end{aligned}$
or $U_1 < U_2$ or $U_2>U_1$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q:
If speed (V), acceleration (A) and force (F) are considered

as fundamental units, the dimension of Young's modulus

will be :
questionIds.solution:
Let [Y] = [V] $^{a}[\mathrm{~F}]^{\mathrm{b}}[\mathrm{A}]^{\mathrm{c}}$

$\left[\mathrm{ML}^{-1} \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]=\left[\mathrm{LT}^{-1}\right]^{\mathrm{a}}\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-2}\right]^{\mathrm{b}}\left[\mathrm{LT}^{-2}\right]^{\mathrm{c}}$

$\left[\mathrm{ML}^{-1} \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]=\left[\mathrm{M}^{b} \mathrm{~L}^{\mathrm{a}+b+\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{T}^{-\mathrm{a}-2 \mathrm{~b}-2 \mathrm{c}}\right]$

Comparing power both side of similar terms we get,

$b=1, a+b+c=-1,-a-2 b-2 c=-2$

solving above equations we get

$a=-4, b=1, c=2$

so $[\mathrm{Y}]=\left[\mathrm{V}^{-4} \mathrm{FA}^{2}\right]=\left[\mathrm{V}^{-4} \mathrm{~A}^{2} \mathrm{~F}\right]$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: The graph between angle of deviation <math> <mrow> <mfenced close=")" open="(" separators=","> <mrow> <mi>δ</mi> </mrow> </mfenced> </mrow> </math> and angle of incidence <math> <mrow> <mfenced close=")" open="(" separators=","> <mrow> <mi>i</mi> </mrow> </mfenced> </mrow> </math> for a triangular prism is represented by :
questionIds.solution:
We know that angle of deviation depend upon the angle of incidence. If we determine experimentally the angle of deviation corresponding to different angles of incidence then and then plot i (on x-axis) and δ (on y-axis), we get a curve as shown in figure.

Clearly, if angle of incidence is gradually increased from a small value, the angle of deviation first decreases, becomes minimum for a particular angle of incidence and then begins to increase.

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: Starting from the origin, a body oscillates simple harmonically with a period of $2 \mathrm{~s}$. After what time will its kinetic energy be $75 \%$ of the total energy?
questionIds.solution:
$\frac{1}{2} m v^2=\frac{3}{4}\left(\frac{1}{2} m v_{\max }^2\right)$
$\mathrm{A}^2 \omega^2 \cos ^2 \omega \mathrm{t} \Rightarrow \frac{3}{4} \mathrm{~A}^2 \omega^2$
$\cos \omega \mathrm{t}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$
$\omega t=\frac{\pi}{6} \Rightarrow t=\frac{1}{6}$ sec

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q:
When both jaws of vernier callipers touch each other, zero mark of the vernier scale is right to zero mark of main scale, $4^{\text {th }}$ mark on vernier scale coincides with certain mark on the main scale. While measuring the length of a cylinder, observer observes 15 divisions on main scale and $5^{\text {th }}$ division of vernier scale coincides with a main scale division. Measured length of cylinder is $\_\_\_\_$ mm.
(Least count of Vernier calliper $=0.1 \mathrm{~mm}$)
questionIds.solution:
Given Least Count ($LC$) = $0.1$ mm.
When jaws touch, the zero of the vernier scale is to the right of the main scale zero, indicating a positive zero error.
Zero error ($e$) = $+ (n \times LC)$, where $n$ is the coinciding vernier division.
$e = +(4 \times 0.1) = +0.4$ mm.
While measuring the cylinder:
Main Scale Reading ($MSR$) = $15$ divisions.
Assuming $1$ MSD = $1$ mm (standard for $LC = 0.1$ mm), $MSR = 15$ mm.
Vernier Scale Reading ($VSR$) = $5 \times LC = 5 \times 0.1 = 0.5$ mm.
Observed Reading = $MSR + VSR = 15 + 0.5 = 15.5$ mm.
True Measured Length = Observed Reading $-$ Zero Error
True Length = $15.5 - 0.4 = 15.1$ mm.

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questionIds.q:
In two different experiments, an object of mass 5 kg moving with a speed of 25 ms-1 hits two different walls and comes to rest within
(i) 3 second, (ii) 5 seconds, respectively.
Choose the correct option out of the following :
options: Impulse and average force acting on the object will be same for both the cases.
Impulse will be same for both the cases but the average force will be different.
Average force will be same for both the cases but the impulse will be different.
Average force and impulse will be different for both the cases.
questionIds.solution:
Impulse = Change in momentum

I=ΔP

Therefore, in both the cases the impulse is same.

Average force is defined as,

Favg =ΔPΔt

Since the time in the two cases is different, the average force will be different.

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: Force between two point charges <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> placed in vacuum at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math> apart is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math>. Force between them when placed in a medium having dielectric <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>r</mi><mn>5</mn></mfrac></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math> apart will be:
questionIds.solution:
Using Coulomb's law, we can write:

In air F=14πε0q1q2r2

In medium F'=14πKε0q1q2r'2=14π5ε025q1q2r2=5F

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: The torque of a force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> about the origin is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>τ</mi></math>. If the force acts on a particle whose position vector is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>τ</mi></math> will be
questionIds.solution:
Using the determinant rule to find the torque

 τ→=r→×F→=i^j^k^22153-7

=i^-14-3-j^-14-5+k^6-10

Thus, the value of τ→=-17i^+19j^-4k^

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: On a linear temperature scale $\mathrm{Y}$, water freezes at $-160^{\circ} \mathrm{Y}$ and boils at $-50^{\circ} \mathrm{Y}$. On this $\mathrm{Y}$ scale, a temperature of $340 \mathrm{~K}$ would be read as : (water freezes at $273 \mathrm{~K}$ and boils at $373 \mathrm{~K}$ )
questionIds.solution:
Reading on any scale - LFP
UFP - LFP
$=$ constant for all scales
$\frac{340-273}{373-273}=\frac{{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{y}-(-160)}{-50-(-160)}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{67}{100}=\frac{\mathrm{y}+160}{110}$
$\therefore \quad y=-86.3^{\circ} \mathrm{y}$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: Eight copper wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math> and diameter d are joined in parallel to form a single composite conductor of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>. If a single copper wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>l</mi></math> have the same resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>R</mi></mfenced></math> then its diameter will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math>.
questionIds.solution:
Each wire has resistance, r=ρ4lπd2

Eight wire in parallel, then equivalent resistance is

R=r8=ρl2πd2

Single copper wire of length 2l has resistance R=ρ2l×4πd12.

Comparing both equations, we get,

ρ8lπd12=ρl2πd2

⇒d1=4d

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: Identify the solar cell characteristics from the following options :
questionIds.solution:
The I-V curve for solar cell is available in the fourth quadrant, i.e. where current is negative, which means that current I is supplied by the solar cell and not drawn by it.

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q:
The energy released in the fusion of $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ of hydrogen deep in the sun is $E_H$ and the energy released in the fission of $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ of ${ }^{235} \mathrm{U}$ is $E_U$. The ratio $\frac{E_H}{E_U}$ is approximately:
(Consider the fusion reaction as $4 \mid H+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow{ }_2^4 \mathrm{He}+2 v+6 \gamma+26.7 \mathrm{MeV}$, energy released in the fission reaction of ${ }^{235} \mathrm{U}$ is $200 \mathrm{MeV}$ per fission nucleus and $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}=$ $\left.6.023 \times 10^{23}\right)$
questionIds.solution:
In each fusion reaction, $4{ }_1^1 \mathrm{H}$ nucleus are used. 
Energy released per Nuclei of ${ }_1^1 \mathrm{H}=\frac{26.7}{4} \mathrm{MeV}$
$\therefore$ Energy released by $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ hydrogen $\left(\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{H}}\right)$
$=\frac{2000}{1} \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}} \times \frac{26.7}{4} \mathrm{MeV}$
$\qquad \qquad\quad \&$
$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \text { Energy released by } 2 \mathrm{~kg} \text { Vranium }\left(\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{V}}\right) \\ & =\frac{2000}{235} \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}} \times 200 \mathrm{MeV}\end{aligned}$
So,
$\frac{E_H}{E_V}=235 \times \frac{26.7}{4 \times 200}=7.84$
$\therefore$ Approximately close to 7.62

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A solid sphere of radius 10 cm is rotating about an axis which is at a distance 15 cm from its centre. The radius of gyration about this axis is $\sqrt{n} \mathrm{~cm}$. The value of $n$ is
questionIds.solution:
Using the parallel axis theorem for radius of gyration:
$k^2 = k_{cm}^2 + d^2$ where $k_{cm}$ is the radius of gyration about the center of mass and $d$ is the distance from the center.
For a solid sphere:
$I_{center} = \frac{2}{5}MR^2$, so $k_{cm}^2 = \frac{2}{5}R^2$
With $R = 10$ cm and $d = 15$ cm:
$k^2 = \frac{2}{5}(10)^2 + (15)^2 = 40 + 225 = 265$
Since $k = \sqrt{n}$, we have $n = 265$.

questionIds.q: A block <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> takes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> to slide down a frictionless incline of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math>, kept inside a lift going up with uniform velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math>. questionIds.solution:
As per the information given in the question the lift is moving upward with uniform velocity. Therefore, the frame of reference attached to the lift is an inertial frame of reference. The acceleration of the block down the incline will be,

a=gsinθ

Using equation of motion for constant acceleration,

s=ut+12at2

⇒l=12gsin30°22       ...1

When the inclination is changed to 45°,

l=12gsin45°t2       ...2

Equating the above equations,

12gsin30°22=12gsin45°t2 

⇒124=12t2⇒t=22=1.68 s

questionIds.q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> of an alkane requires <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> of oxygen for its complete combustion. at t=0,            P0                     0              0

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q:
The number of atomic orbitals from the following having 5 radial nodes is
7s, 7p, 6s, 8p, 8d
questionIds.solution:
The number of radial nodes for an orbital with principal quantum number n and azimuthal quantum number l is given by the expression:

Number of radial nodes = n - l - 1

Using this expression, we can determine the number of radial nodes for each of the given atomic orbitals:

7s:n=7,l=0  7-0-1=6
7p:n=7,l=1  7-1-1=5
6s:n=6,l=0  6-0-1=5
8p:n=8,l=1  8-1-1=6
8d:n=8,l=2  8-2-1=5

Therefore, the atomic orbitals with 5 radial nodes are 7p, 6s, and 8d.

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: A cup of water at $5^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ (system) is placed in a microwave oven and the oven is turned on for one minute during which the water begins to boil. Which of the following option is true ?
questionIds.solution:
For water heating in a microwave:
Heat (q): The microwave radiates electromagnetic energy that is absorbed by water molecules, so q = +ve
Work (w): As water heats and boils, it expands against atmospheric pressure. The system does work on the surroundings, so w = -ve
Internal Energy (ΔU): From the first law, ΔU = q + w. Since heat absorbed (+ve) is much larger in magnitude than work done against atmosphere (-ve), the net change is ΔU = +ve (confirmed by temperature increase from 5°C to boiling)
Therefore: q = +ve, w = -ve, ΔU = +ve

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: The correct order of basicity of oxides of vanadium is
questionIds.solution:
V2O3: In, V2O3, the oxidation state of V is as follows

2(x) + 3(-2) = 0

⇒ 2x - 6 = 0

⇒ x = 62 = 3

The oxidation state of V in V2O3 is +3. The vanadium is present in its lowest oxidation state. The vanadium tends to donate the pair of electrons. Hence, it is basic oxide.

V2O5: In V2O5, the oxidation state of V is as follows

2(x) + 5(-2) = 0

⇒ 2(x) - 10 = 0

⇒x = 102 = 5

The oxidation state of V in V2O5 is +5. The Vanadium tends to donate and accept the pair of electrons. It is amphoteric oxide.

V2O4: In V2O4, the oxidation state of V is as follows

2(x) + 4(-2) = 0

⇒ 2(x) - 8 = 0

⇒ x = 82 = 4

The oxidation state of V in V2O4 is +4. The Vanadium is present in its other low oxidation state. The Vanadium tends to donate and accept the pair of electrons. It is amphoteric oxide and less basic than V2O3, more basic than V2O5.

So, the correct order of basicity of oxides of vanadium is V2O3>V2O4>V2O5.

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: The correct stability order of carbocations is
questionIds.solution:
CH33C+-Tertiary carbocationCH32C+H-Secondary carbocationCH3-C+H2-Primary carbocationC+H3-Methyl carbocation

Stability of carbocations depends upon the electron releasing groups. The given carbocations stability can be explained by hyperconjugation effect. More the number of alpha hydrogen, more are number of hyperconjugation structures, hence, more is the stability of carbocation.

Thus, the observed order of stability for carbocations is as follows: tertiary > secondary > primary > methyl

Hence, option C is correct.

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q:
The ascending order of acidity of –OH group in the following compounds is :

      (A) Bu – OH

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
questionIds.solution:
The decrease in electron density of the O-H bond of o,p-dinitrophenol, the polarity of O-H bond decreases. The electron withdrawing group -NO2), withdraws electrons and disperses the negative charge. Therefore, -NO2 group stabilizes the phenoxide ion. Hence, o,p-dinitrophenol is more acidic than p-nitrophenol and phenol.

OMe is an electron donating group, hence it increases electron density, there why making it less acidic.

Bu-OH is an aliphatic alcohol. In phenoxide ion the negative charge is delocalized within the ring structure which makes it more stable than the aliphatic alcohols. In aliphatic alcohols there is no such delocalization stabilization. Therefore, phenols are more acidic and the resulting phenoxide ion is stabilized because of resonance.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The mean age of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn></math> teachers in a school is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn></math> years. A teacher retires at the age of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn></math> years and a new teacher is appointed in his place. If the mean age of the teachers in this school now is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>39</mn></math> years, then the age (in years) of the newly appointed teacher is
questionIds.solution:
∑xi25=40 and ∑xi-60+N25=39

Let age of newly appointed teacher is N

⇒1000-60+N=975

⇒N=35 year

questionIds.q: The equation of one of the straight lines which passes through the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>)</mo></math> and makes an angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>(</mo><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mo>)</mo></math> with the straight line, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mi>x</mi></math> is
questionIds.solution:
y=mx+c

3=m+c

2=m-321+32m

⇒6m+2=±m-32

from Positive sign

⇒5m=-42 ⇒m=-425

from negative sign

⇒6m+2=-m+32

⇒7 m=22⇒m=227

According to options take m=-425

So y=-42x5+3+425

42x+5y-(15+42)=0

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If the point $(1, a)$ lies between the straight lines $x+y=1$ and $2(x+y)=3$ then a lies in interval
questionIds.solution: Since, $(1, a)$ lies between $x+y=1$ and $2(x+y)=3$ $\therefore$ Put $x=1$ in $2(x+y)=3$. We get the range of $y$. Thus, $$ 2(1+y)=3 \Rightarrow y=\frac{3}{2}-1=\frac{1}{2} $$ Thus ' $a$ 'lies in $\left(0, \frac{1}{2}\right)$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If the sum of the deviations of <math><mn>50</mn></math> observations from <math><mn>30</mn></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math>, then the mean of these observations is :
questionIds.solution:
Let the observations be x1, x2, ..., x50.

Given x1-30+x2-30+.....+x50-30=50

⇒ x1+x2+.....+x50=50+30×50=1550

Now, Mean =x1+x2+....+x5050=155050=31.

questionIds.q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mo>∫</mo><mn>0</mn><mi>π</mi></msubsup><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><msup><mi>e</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mi>β</mi><mi>e</mi></mfrac><msubsup><mo>∫</mo><mn>0</mn><mn>1</mn></msubsup><msqrt><mi>t</mi></msqrt><msup><mi>e</mi><mi>t</mi></msup><mi>d</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi></math> is equal to
questionIds.solution:
Let I=∫0πsin3xe-sin2xdx

⇒I=2∫0π2sin3xe-sin2xdx applying ∫0afx=2∫0a2fx when fx=fa-x

=2∫0π2sinx1-cos2xe-sin2xdx

=2∫0π2sinxe-sin2xdx-∫0π22sinxcosx·cosxe-sin2xdx

=2∫0π2sinxe-sin2x dx+∫0π2cosx⏟I·e-sin2x-sin2x⏟IIdx

=2∫0π2sinxe-sin2xdx+cosxe-sin2x0π2+∫0π2sinxe-sin2xdx

=3∫0π2sinxe-sin2xdx-1

=32∫-10eαdα1+α-1  Put-sin2x=α

=32e∫01exxdx-1Put 1+α=x

=32e∫01ex1xdx-1=32e2xex01-∫012xexdx-1

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Tangents drawn from the point $(-8,0)$ to the parabola $y^2=8 x$ touch the parabola at $P$ and $Q$. If $F$ is the focus of the parabola, then the area of the triangle $P F Q$ (in sq. units) is equal to
questionIds.solution:
Equation of the chord of contact $P Q$ is given by:
$$
T=0
$$
or $T \equiv y y_1-4\left(x+x_1\right)$,
where $\left(x_1, y_1\right) \equiv(-8,0)$
$\therefore$ Equation becomes: $x=8$
$\&$ Chord of contact is $x=8$
$\therefore$ Coordinates of point $P$ and $Q$ are $(8,8)$ and $(8,-8)$
and focus of the parabola is $F(2,0)$
$\therefore$ Area of triangle $P Q F=\frac{1}{2} \times(8-2) \times(8+8)$ $=48$ sq. units

questionIds.q: If the inverse trigonometric functions take principal values, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>10</mn></mfrac><mi>cos</mi><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>4</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mn>2</mn><mn>5</mn></mfrac><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>4</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to

So, cos-1310costan-143+25sintan-143

questionIds.q: The sum of the squares of the lengths of the chords intercepted on the circle, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi> </mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>16</mn><mo>,</mo></math> by the lines, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>n</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi> </mi><mi>n</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>N</mi><mo>,</mo></math> where <math><mi>N</mi></math> is the set of all natural numbers is:
questionIds.solution:
The length of the perpendicular from centre 0,0  to the line x+y=n is 0+0-n12+I2=n2

Length of intercept =242-n22=64-2n2

⇒ Possible values of n are 1,2,….5

⇒ Sum of the square of lengths of intercepts

=Σn=1564-2n2=64×5-2Σn=15n2

=320-2Σn=15n2=320-110=210

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If domain of the function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mn>6</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfrac></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfrac></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∪</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>γ</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>δ</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>α</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>β</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>γ</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>δ</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
questionIds.solution:
We need to find the domain of the function loge6x2+5x+12x-1+cos-12x2-3x+43x-5.

⇒6x2+5x+12x-1>0 .....(1) and

⇒ -1≤2x2-3x+43x-5≤1......(2)
Now From 1,
⇒6x2+5x+12x-1>0

⇒(3x+1)(2x+1)(2x-1)>0

Now we get the common region here as
⇒x∈-12,-13∪12,∞.......(a)

From 2 we get that
⇒-1≤2x2-3x+43x-5≤1

⇒2x2-3x+43x-5≥-1......3

and 2x2-3x+43x-5≤1.....4

From 3 we get
⇒2x2-3x+43x-5+1≥0

⇒x∈-12,12∪53,∞.....b

From 4 we get
⇒2x2-3x+43x-5-1≤0

⇒2x2-6x+93x-5≤0

As D=-62-429<0 and 2>0, we observe that 2x2-6x+9>0 ∀x∈ℝ
⇒13x-5≤0  ∵2x2-6x+9>0∀x∈R

⇒  x∈-∞,53   ......c

⇒ Intersection of (a), (b) and (c) gives us
x∈-12,-13∪12,12.

On comparing this with α,β∪γ,δ,
⇒α2+β2+γ2+δ2=109
⇒18α2+β2+γ2+δ2=20.

Hence this is the required answer.

questionIds.q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mi>tan</mi><msup><mn>1</mn><mo>°</mo></msup></mrow></mfenced><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mfenced><mn>123</mn></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo> </mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mfenced><mn>1234</mn></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>tan</mi><msup><mn>1</mn><mo>°</mo></msup></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math>, then the least value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mfrac><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></math> is
questionIds.solution:
Given,

fx=tan1°x+loge123x loge1234-tan1°, x>0

Now, let tan1°=a, loge123=b and loge1234=c

So, fx=ax+bcx-a

⇒ffx=afx+bcfx-a

⇒ffx=aax+bcx-a+bcax+bcx-a-a

⇒ffx=a2x+ab+bcx-aacx+bc-acx-a=a2x+bcxbc+a2=x

⇒ffx+ff4x=x+4x

∵ x>0, then least value =x·4x=2

questionIds.q: On the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mn>8</mn></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></math> be a point in the second quadrant such that the tangent at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></math> to the ellipse is perpendicular to the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mo>'</mo></msup></math> be the foci of the ellipse and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>e</mi></math> be its eccentricity. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is the area of the triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>SPS</mi><mo>'</mo></msup></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>5</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>·</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is
questionIds.solution:
We have an ellipse x28+y24=1, we have a general point P(8cosθ,2sinθ)

Equation of tangent at point p

x8cosθ+y2sinθ-1=0

Slope =-12cotθ

Given that, tangent at P is perpendicular to the line x+2y=0.

So, product of their slopes are perpendicular ⇒-12cotθ×-12=-1

⇒-12cotθ=2

⇒cotθ=-22

⇒cosθ=-223,sinθ=13

So, point P-83,23

A=12×2ae×23

Where, e=a2-b2a2=8-48=12

∴A=12×2×22×12×23=43So, 5-e2A=5-12×43=6

questionIds.solution:
Let d1 be the common difference of the A.P. x1,x2,…, then x8-x3=5 d1=12⇒

⇒x5=x3+2 d1=8+2×125=12.8

Let d2 be the common difference of the other sequence then

5 d2=120-18=-340⇒d2

⇒1 h10=1 h7+3 d2=1200⇒h10=200

⇒x5·h10=12.8×200=2560

questionIds.q: If the real part of the complex number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>cos</mi><mi>θ</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>i</mi><mi>sin</mi><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>5</mn></mfrac></math> for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi><mo>∈</mo><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>π</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>,</mo></math> then the value of the integral <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mo>∫</mo><mn>0</mn><mi>θ</mi></msubsup><mi>sin</mi><mi>x</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi>x</mi></math> is equal to:
questionIds.solution:

Hence,

Rez=2sin2θ24sin4θ2+16sin2θ2cos2θ2

⇒12sin2θ2+8cos2θ2=15

⇒12sin2θ2+8cos2θ2=15

⇒sin2θ2+4cos2θ2=52

⇒1-cos2θ2+4cos2θ2=52

⇒3cos2θ2=32

⇒cos2θ2=12

⇒θ2=nπ±π4, n∈Z

⇒θ=2nπ±π2

For θ∈(0,π), θ=π2.

Hence,

∴∫0θsinθdθ=∫0π2sinθdθ=-cosθ0π2

=-0-1

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let a circle $C$ have its centre in the first quadrant, intersect the coordinate axes at exactly three points and cut off equal intercepts from the coordinate axes. If the length of the chord of $C$ on the line $x + y = 1$ is $\sqrt{14}$, then the square of the radius of $C$ is _______.
questionIds.solution:
Let the equation of the circle be $x^2 + y^2 + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0$.

Since the circle intersects the coordinate axes at exactly three points, it must pass through the origin and intersect each axis at one other distinct point. Thus, $c = 0$.

The lengths of the intercepts on the x-axis and y-axis are $2\sqrt{g^2 - c} = 2|g|$ and $2\sqrt{f^2 - c} = 2|f|$.

Given that the intercepts are equal, we have $|g| = |f|$.

The centre of the circle is $(-g, -f)$. Since it lies in the first quadrant, $-g > 0$ and $-f > 0$. Therefore, $g = f = -a$ for some $a > 0$.

The centre is $(a, a)$ and the radius is $r = \sqrt{a^2 + a^2} = a\sqrt{2}$.

The distance $d$ from the centre $(a, a)$ to the line $x + y - 1 = 0$ is given by:

$d = \dfrac{|a + a - 1|}{\sqrt{1^2 + 1^2}} = \dfrac{|2a - 1|}{\sqrt{2}}$

The length of the chord on the given line is $2\sqrt{r^2 - d^2} = \sqrt{14}$.

Squaring both sides, we get:

$4(r^2 - d^2) = 14 \Rightarrow r^2 - d^2 = \dfrac{7}{2}$

Substituting $r^2 = 2a^2$ and $d^2 = \dfrac{(2a - 1)^2}{2}$:

$2a^2 - \dfrac{(2a - 1)^2}{2} = \dfrac{7}{2}$

$4a^2 - (4a^2 - 4a + 1) = 7$

$4a - 1 = 7 \Rightarrow 4a = 8 \Rightarrow a = 2$

The square of the radius of the circle is $r^2 = 2a^2 = 2(2)^2 = 8$.

Answer: $8$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>→</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>∨</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is false, then the truth values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> are, respectively
questionIds.solution:
p→q has truth value false only when p is true & q is false. Hence, truth value of p is true.

Now, ~p∨~q is false only when ~p & ~q both are false.

Hence, p and q are both true.

questionIds.q: The number of natural numbers less than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7000</mn></math> which can be formed by using the digits <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>7</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>9</mn></math> (repetition of digits allowed) is equal to:
questionIds.solution:
∴ Total number formed less than

questionIds.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> be a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><mn>3</mn></math> real matrix such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mfenced><mtable columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mtable columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced><mo>;</mo><mi>A</mi><mfenced><mtable columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mtable><mtr><mtd><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mfenced><mtable columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mtable columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>2</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mi>T</mi></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> is an identity matrix of order <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math>, then the system <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>A</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>I</mi></mrow></mfenced><mi>X</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mtable columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><mn>4</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced></math> has
questionIds.solution:
Let the matrix be A=a1b1c1a2b2c2a3b3c3

Now A001=c1c2c3=112

⇒  c1=1,c2=1,c3=2

A101=c1+a1c2+a2c3+a3=-101

⇒  a1=-2,a2=-1,a3=-1

A110=a1+b1a2+b2a3+b3=110

⇒  b1=3,b2=2,b3=1

So matrix A=-231-121-112

⇒A-2I=-431-101-110

i.e. A-2 I=0

Now X=x1x2x3

So,   -431-101-110x1x2x3=411

-4x1+3x2+x3=4     ...1

-x1+x3=1       ...2

-x1+x2=1   ...3

Solving the above system of equations we get infinite solutions.

questionIds.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>D</mi></math> be the centroid of the triangle with vertices <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> , <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> . Let <math><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></math> be the point of intersection of the lines <math><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn></math> and <math><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> . Then, the line passing through the points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>D</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></math> also passes through the point:
questionIds.solution:
Centroid x1+x2+x33,y1+y2+y33

D2,2
Point of intersection P-15,25
Equation of line DP 8x-11y+6=0.

Hence, -9,-6 satisfy above equation.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfrac></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mo>⁡</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then the value of the integral <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><munderover><mo>∫</mo><mrow><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></munderover><mi>g</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi></math> is
questionIds.solution:
Given,

fx=2-xcosx2+xcosx, g(x)=loge⁡x

⇒gfx=loge2-xcosx2+xcosx

⇒gf-x=⁡loge2-(-x)cos⁡(-x)2+(-x)cos⁡(-x)

⇒gf-x=loge2+xcosx2-xcosx

⇒gf-x=-log2-xcosx2+xcosx

⇒gf-x=–g(fx)

Hence, g(fx) is an odd function.

By using the property of definite integration, ∫-aafxdx=2∫0afxdx,  f-x=fx0,f-x=-fx, we can write
∫-π4π4g(f(x))dx=0=loge⁡1

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>cos</mi><mfrac><mrow><mn>7</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>6</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>tan</mi><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
questionIds.solution:
Given, sin-1sin2π3+cos-1cos7π6+tan-1tan3π4

Now simplifying each term we get, 

sin-1sin2π3=π-2π3=π3

cos-1cos7π6=2π-7π6=5π6

tan-1tan3π4=3π4-π=-π4

sin-1sin2π3+cos-1cos7π6+tan-1tan3π4

=π3+5π6-π4

=11π12
