questionIds.q: If the sum <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>3</mn><msup><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mn>5</mn><mrow><msup><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mn>2</mn><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mn>7</mn><mrow><msup><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mn>2</mn><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mn>3</mn><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mo>....</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">.</mi><mo>+</mo></math> up to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> terms is equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>k</mi><mn>21</mn></mfrac><mi>,</mi></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> is equal to
questionIds.solution:
S20=312+512+22+712+22+32+....+upto 20terms

=Σr=1202r+112+22+32+.... r2

=Σr=1202r+1r6r+12r+1

=6Σr=1201rr+1

=6Σr=1201r-1r+1

=611-12+12-13+13-14+....+120-121

=61-121

=6×2021

=12021

=k21

k=120

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q:
For the system of equations

x+y+z=6

x+2y+αz=10

x+3y+5z=β, which one of the following is NOT true?
options: System has a unique solution for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>β</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>14</mn></math>
System has infinitely many solutions for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>β</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>14</mn></math>
System has a unique solution for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>β</mi><mo>≠</mo><mn>14</mn></math>
questionIds.solution:
Given, the system of equations

x+y+z=6

x+2y+αz=10

x+3y+5z=β

Now finding,

△=11112α135

⇒△=110-3α-5-α+3-2

⇒△=6-2α

Now for unique solution, △≠0⇒α≠3

So, for unique solution ⇒α≠3

But in option D, system has unique solution for α=3 which is completely wrong.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><munderover><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mi>r</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow><mn>22</mn></munderover><mmultiscripts><mi>C</mi><mi>r</mi><none></none><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mn>22</mn></mmultiscripts><mo>·</mo><mmultiscripts><mi>C</mi><mi>r</mi><none></none><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mn>23</mn></mmultiscripts></math> is
questionIds.solution:
Let, S=∑r=022Cr22·Cr23

Now using Crn=Cn-rn we get,

S=∑r=022Cr22·C23-r23

⇒S=C022·C2323+C122·C2223+............C2222·C123

Which is sum of coefficient of x23 in the expansion of 1+x22·1+x23 or 1+x23+22=1+x45

Hence, ∑r=022Cr22·C23-r23=C2345

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: All possible numbers are formed using the digits <math><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>4</mn><mi> </mi></math> taken all at a time. The number of such numbers in which the odd digits occupy even places is
questionIds.solution:
Odd digits: 1, 1, 3
Even digits: 2, 2, 2, 2, 4, 4
The number of ways of placing odd digits at even position = C34×3!2!=4×3=12
The number of ways of placing even digits =6!4! 2!=15 
∴ Total number of ways =12×15=180

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If $\sum_{r=1}^{30} \frac{r^2\left({ }^{30} C_r\right)^2}{{ }^{30} C_{r-1}}=\alpha \times 2^{29}$, then $\alpha$ is equal to ______
questionIds.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^{30} \frac{\mathrm{r}^2\left({ }^{30} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}\right)^2}{{ }^{30} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}-1}} \\ & =\sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^{30} \mathrm{r}^2\left(\frac{31-\mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{r}}\right) \cdot \frac{30!}{\mathrm{r}!(30-\mathrm{r})!} \\ & \left(\because \frac{{ }^{30} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}}{{ }^{30} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}-1}}=\frac{30-\mathrm{r}+1}{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{31-\mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{r}}\right)\end{aligned}$


$\begin{aligned} & =\sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^{30} \frac{(31-\mathrm{r}) 30!}{(\mathrm{r}-1)!(30-\mathrm{r})!} \\ & =30 \sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^{30} \frac{(31-\mathrm{r}) 29!}{(\mathrm{r}-1)!(30-\mathrm{r})!} \\ & =30 \sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^{30}(30-\mathrm{r}+1)^{29} \mathrm{C}_{30-\mathrm{r}} \\ & =30\left(\sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^{30}(31-\mathrm{r})^{29} \mathrm{C}_{30-\mathrm{r}}+\sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^{30}{ }^{29} \mathrm{C}_{30-\mathrm{r}}\right) \\ & =30\left(29 \times 2^{28}+2^{29}\right)=30(29+2) 2^{28} \\ & =15 \times 31 \times 2^{29} \\ & =465\left(2^{29}\right) \\ & \alpha=465\end{aligned}$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q:
Let $f:(0, \pi) \rightarrow \mathbf{R}$ be a function given by $f(x)=$ \(\left\{\begin{array}{cc}\left(\frac{8}{7}\right)^{\frac{\tan 8 x}{\tan 7 x}}, & 0 < x < \frac{\pi}{2} \\ \mathrm{a}-8, & x=\frac{\pi}{2} \\ (1+|\cot x|)^{\frac{\mathrm{b}}{}|\tan x|}, & \frac{\pi}{2} < x < \pi\end{array}\right.\)
where $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b} \in \mathbf{Z}$. If $f$ is continuous at $x=\frac{\pi}{2}$, then $\mathrm{a}^2+\mathrm{b}^2$ is equal to
questionIds.solution:
LHL at $\mathrm{x}=\frac{\pi}{2}$ $\lim _{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{2}}\left(\frac{8}{7}\right)^{\frac{\tan 8 x}{\tan 7 x}}=\left(\frac{8}{7}\right)^0=1$
RHL at $\mathrm{x}=\frac{\pi}{2}$ $\begin{aligned} & \lim _{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{2}}(1+|\cot x|)^{\frac{b}{\operatorname{lan}} \tan x} \\ & =e^{\lim _{x-\frac{\pi}{2}}|\cot x| \frac{b}{a}|\tan x|}=e^{\frac{b}{a}} \end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 1=a-8=e^{\frac{b}{a}} \\ & \Rightarrow a=9, b=0 \\ & \Rightarrow a^2+b^2=81\end{aligned}$

Choose the incorrect option from the following:

S=α2βℓnμkRJβ2+3
S=QT=Joule k-1
α2β=Joule k-1
PV=nRT  μkRJβ2=1

R= Joule k
⇒R= Joule k,k= Joule R
⇒β= Joule k
α2β= Joule k
⇒α= dimensionless

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A long solenoid has 200 turns per $\mathrm{cm}$ and carries a current $\mathrm{i}$. The magnetic field at its centre is $6.28 \times 10^{-2}$ Weber $/ \mathrm{m}^2$. Another long solenoid has 100 turns per $\mathrm{cm}$ and it carries a current $\mathrm{i} / 3$. The value of the magnetic field at its centre is
questionIds.solution: $B_2=\frac{B_1 n_2 i_2}{n_1 i_1}=\frac{\left(6.28 \times 10^{-2}\right)(100 \times i / 3)}{200(i)}=1.05 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{m}^2$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: Two light beams of intensities <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>I</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mi>I</mi></math> interfere on a screen. The phase difference between these beams on the screen at point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is zero and at point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi></math>. The difference of resultant intensities, at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>, will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math>.
questionIds.solution:
The resultant intensity is given by Inet =I1+I2+2I1I2cosϕ

Now, for Imax, phase angle is ϕ=0 and for Imin, phase angle is ϕ=π.

Thus, Imax=I1+I22=9I+4I2=25I

And Imin=I1-I22=9I-4I2=I

The difference of resultant intensities is Imax-Imin=25I-I=24I

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: The energy released if hydrogen atoms are combined to form $^{4}_{2}\text{He}$ is __________ MeV. (Take binding energies per nucleon of $^{2}_{1}\text{H}$ and $^{4}_{2}\text{He}$ as $1.1$ MeV and $7.2$ MeV, respectively)
questionIds.solution:
The fusion reaction is given by:

$2 ^{2}_{1}\text{H} \rightarrow ^{4}_{2}\text{He}$

Binding energy of one $^{2}_{1}\text{H}$ nucleus = $2 \times 1.1 = 2.2$ MeV

Total initial binding energy = $2 \times 2.2 = 4.4$ MeV

Binding energy of $^{4}_{2}\text{He}$ nucleus = $4 \times 7.2 = 28.8$ MeV

Energy released $\Delta E$ = Final binding energy - Initial binding energy

$\Delta E = 28.8 - 4.4 = 24.4$ MeV

Answer: $24.4$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: The displacement of a damped harmonic oscillator is given by <math><mi>x</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>0.1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></msup><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">φ</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mrow><mo>.</mo></math> Here <math><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></math> is in seconds. The time taken for its amplitude of vibration to drop to half of its initial value is close to:
questionIds.solution:
Amplitude A=A0e-kt
From given condition, A02=A0e-0.1×t
⇒ln2=0.1×t
t=ln20.1=0.6930.10=6.935≈7 s

questionIds.q: Radiation coming from transitions <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> of hydrogen atoms fall on <math><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> ions in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> states. The possible transition of helium ions as they absorb energy from the radiation is:
questionIds.solution:
E=13.6111-122 eV  ...(1)
For Helium:
E=13.6221n12-1n22 eV ...(2)
From equation (1) and (2)
13.6 34=13.6 4 1n12-1n22
1n12-1n22=316
From option, n1=2 and n2=4  are satisfying the equation.

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: Train A is moving along two parallel rail tracks towards north with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>72</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and train <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is moving towards south with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>108</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Velocity of train <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> with respect to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and velocity of ground with respect to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> are (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>):
questionIds.solution:
VA=72 km h-1=72×10003600=20 m s-1 and 

VB=-108 km h-1=-108×10003600=-30 m s-1

Velocity of B w.r.t. A:

VB/A=-30-20=– 50 m s-1

Velocity of ground w.r.t. B:

VG/B=0--30=30 m s-1

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: The pitch of the screw gauge is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and there are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> divisions on the circular scale. When nothing is put in between the jaws, the zero of the circular scale lies <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math> divisions below the reference line. When a wire is placed between the jaws, the first linear scale division is clearly visible while <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>72</mn><mtext>nd </mtext></msup></math> division on circular scale coincides with the reference line. The radius of the wire is
questionIds.solution:
Least count =1 mm100=0.01 mm

zero error =+8×LC=+0.08 mm

True reading (Diameter)

=1 mm+72×LC- (Zero error)

=1 mm+72×0.01 mm-0.08 mm

=1.72 mm-0.08 mm

=1.64 mm

therefore, radius =1.642=0.82 mm.

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: The focal length of the objective and the eyepiece of a telescope are $50 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $5 \mathrm{~cm}$ respectively. If the telescope is focussed for distinct vision on a scale distant $2 \mathrm{~m}$ from its objective, then its magnifying power will be :
questionIds.solution:
Given: $\mathrm{f}_0=50 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{e}}=5 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\mathrm{d}=25 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{u}_0=-200 \mathrm{~cm}$
Magnification $\mathrm{M}=$ ?
As $\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}_0}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}_0}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}_0}$
$$
\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}_0}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}_0}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}_0}=\frac{1}{50}-\frac{1}{200}=\frac{4-1}{200}=\frac{3}{200}
$$
or $\mathrm{v}_0=\frac{200}{3} \mathrm{~cm}$
Now $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{e}}=\mathrm{d}=-25 \mathrm{~cm}$
From, $\frac{1}{v_e}-\frac{1}{u_e}=\frac{1}{f_e}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& -\frac{1}{u_e}=\frac{1}{f_e}-\frac{1}{v_e} \\
& =\frac{1}{5}+\frac{1}{25}=\frac{6}{25}
\end{aligned}
$$
or, $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{e}}=\frac{-25}{6} \mathrm{~cm}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Magnification } \mathrm{M}=\mathrm{M}_0 \times \mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{e}} \\
& =\frac{\mathrm{v}_0}{\mathrm{u}_0} \times \frac{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{e}}}{\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{e}}}=\frac{-200 / 3}{200} \times \frac{-25}{-25 / 6} \\
& =-\frac{1}{3} \times 6=-2
\end{aligned}
$$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q:
Moment of Inertia (M.I.) of four bodies having same mass M and radius 2R are as follows
I1= M.I. of solid sphere about its diameter
I2= M.I. of solid cylinder about its axis
I3= M.I. of solid circular disc about its diameter
I4= M.I. of thin circular ring about its diameter
If 2I2+I3+I4=xI1 then the value of x will be _____ .
questionIds.solution:
∵ 2I2+I3+I4=xI1

Now

For solid sphere about its diameter I1=25MR2,

For solid cylinder about its axis I2=12MR2,

For circular disc about its diameter I3=14MR2

And for circular ring about its diameter I4=12MR2

⇒2MR22+MR24+MR22=x×25MR2

⇒234+12=x×25

⇒2=x×25

⇒x=5

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: For a nucleus of mass number $A$ and radius $R$, the mass density of nucleus can be represented as
questionIds.solution:
The mass density ( $\rho$) of a nucleus is defined as the ratio of its mass to its volume:
$\rho=$ Mass of nucleus / Volume of nucleus
For a spherical nucleus with radius R , the volume is:
$\text { Volume }=(4 / 3) \pi R^3$
The mass of the nucleus is related to the mass number A :
$\text { Mass }-\mathrm{A} \times \mathrm{m} \text { _u }$
(where m_u is the atomic mass unit, approximately $1.66 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}$)
Therefore:
$\rho=\left(\mathrm{A} \times \mathrm{m} \_\mathrm{u}\right) /\left[(4 / 3) \pi \mathrm{R}^3\right]$
Now, there's an important relationship between the mass number A and nuclear radius R. Empirically, it has been found that:
$\mathrm{R}=\mathrm{Ro} * \mathrm{~A}^{\wedge}(1 / 3)$
Where Ro is a constant approximately equal to $1.2 \times 10^{-15} \mathrm{~m}(1.2$ fermi).
Substituting this into the density equation:
$\begin{aligned}
& \rho=\left(\mathrm{A} \times \mathrm{m} \_\mathrm{u}\right) /\left[(4 / 3) \pi\left(\mathrm{Ro} \times \mathrm{A}^{\wedge}(1 / 3)\right)^3\right] \\ & \rho=(\mathrm{A} \times \mathrm{m} \text { _u }) /\left[(4 / 3) \pi \mathrm{Ro}^3 \times \mathrm{A}\right] \\ & \rho=\mathrm{m} \_\mathrm{u} /\left[(4 / 3) \pi \mathrm{Ro}^3\right]
\end{aligned}$
This shows that the nuclear density is approximately constant for all nuclei, regardless of their mass number. This is a fundamental property of nuclear matter known as nuclear saturation density.
The numerical value is:
$\rho=2.3 \times 10^{17} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A flask contains hydrogen and oxygen in the ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn></math> by mass at temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. The ratio of average kinetic energy per molecule of hydrogen and oxygen respectively is :
questionIds.solution:
Average Kinetic Energy per molecule KEavg=32kBT. Therefore, we can conclude that the average kinetic energy of the molecules is directly proportional to the temperature of the gas and is independent of pressure, volume or the nature of the gas.

Hence, required ratio =1:1

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: Which level of the single ionized carbon has the same energy as the ground state energy of hydrogen atom?
questionIds.solution:
Energy of H -atom is E=-13.6Z2/n2 for H-atom Z=1 and for ground state, n=1

⇒E=-13.6×1212=-13.6 eV

Now for carbon atom (single ionised), Z=6

E=-13.6Z2n2=-13.6 (given)

⇒n2=62⇒n=6

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: In an adiabatic process, which of the following statements is true?
options: The molar heat capacity is infinite
Work done by the gas equals the increase in internal energy
The molar heat capacity is zero
The internal energy of the gas decreases as the temperature increases
questionIds.solution:
For adiabatic process, $\mathrm{dQ}=0$
$\therefore$ Molar heat capacity $=0$
$\because \mathrm{dQ}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{dU}=-\mathrm{dW}$
Also $\mathrm{dU}=\frac{\mathrm{f}}{2} \mathrm{nRdT}$
$\therefore$ Only option (3) is correct.

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: The orbital angular momentum of a satellite is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math>, when it is revolving in a circular orbit at height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></math> from earth surface. If the distance of satellite from the earth centre is increased by eight times to its initial value, then the new angular momentum will be
questionIds.solution:
For a satellite revolving around the Earth in a circular orbit, it can be written that

mv2r=GMmr2   ...1 (r=R+h is the distance from the centre of the Earth)

Simplify equation (1) to obtain the linear speed of the satellite.

v=GMr

Thus, the angular momentum of the satellite is given by

L=mvr=mrGMr=mGMr   ...2

Similarly, for distance r' from the centre of the Earth, the angular momentum is given by

L'=mGMr'   ...3

Divide equation (3) by equation (2) to obtain the required angular momentum.

L'L=mGMr'mGMr= r'r   ...4

Now, r'=r+8r=9r

Substitute the known values of the parameters into equation (4) to obtain the required angular momentum.

L'L=9⇒L'=3L

questionIds.q: The resistance per centimeter of a meter bridge wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math>, with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo> </mo><mi>Ω</mi></math> resistance in left gap. Balancing length from left end is at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Ω</mi></math> resistance in right gap. questionIds.solution:
For first case:

For second case:

X2rl'1=252rl'2 ... (ii)

From (i) and (ii), we get

l'1l1=l'2l2 also total length is constant.

Therefore, balance length doesn't change and 

l'1=l1=40 cm

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A cube of metal is subjected to a hydrostatic pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>G</mi><mi>P</mi><mi>a</mi></math>. The percentage change in the length of the side of the cube is close to : (Given bulk modulus of metal, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>P</mi><mi>a</mi></math>)
questionIds.solution:
B=-∆P∆VV
∆VV=-∆PB
Magnitude is given by,
∆VV=∆PB

Volume in terms of length can be represented as,

3×∆LL=∆PB

Therefore,

∆LL=13×∆PB

Substituting the values we get,
∆LL=13×4×1098×1010=160

Percantage change is,
160×100=53=1.67

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q:
The ratio of speeds of electromagnetic waves in vacuum and a medium, having dielectric constant $k=3$ and permeability of $\mu=2 \mu_{0}$, is
($\mu_{0}=$ permeability of vacuum)
questionIds.solution:
Speed of electromagnetic wave in vacuum: $c = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0 \epsilon_0}}$
Speed in a medium with permeability $\mu = 2\mu_0$ and dielectric constant $k = 3$:
$v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu \epsilon}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\mu_0 \times 3\epsilon_0}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6\mu_0\epsilon_0}}$
$v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0\epsilon_0}} = \frac{c}{\sqrt{6}}$
Ratio of speeds: $\frac{c}{v} = \sqrt{6}$
Therefore: $c : v = \sqrt{6} : 1$

questionIds.solution:

f=3+3+48=54

γ=1+2f=2827=1.03

To do so, the person <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> pushes it at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> from the horizontal and requires a minimum force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>F</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub></math>, while the person <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> pulls the box at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> from the horizontal and needs minimum force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>F</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></msub></math>. If the coefficient of friction between the box and the floor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mn>5</mn></mfrac></math>, the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>F</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub><msub><mi>F</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></msub></mfrac></math> is
questionIds.solution:
FAcos30°=μmg+FAsin30°

FBcos60°=μmg-FBsin60°

FAcos30°-μsin30°=μmg

FBcos60°+μsin60°=μmg

Divide the equation,

we get,

⇒FAFB=μmg/cos30°-μsin30°μmg/cos60°+μsin60°=23

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q:
Given below are two statements: Statement I : When speed of liquid is zero everywhere, pressure difference at any two points depends on equation $P_1-P_2=\rho g\left(h_2-h_1\right)$. Statement II : In ventury tube shown $2 \mathrm{gh}=v_1^2-v_2^2$
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct.
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect.
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct.
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect.
questionIds.solution:
Applying Bernoulli's equation $\mathrm{P}_1+\rho \mathrm{gh}_1+\frac{1}{2} \rho \mathrm{v}_1^2=\mathrm{P}_2+\rho \mathrm{gh}_2+\frac{1}{2} \rho \mathrm{v}_2^2$ $\left[h_1 \& h_2\right.$ are height of point from any reference level] Given $\mathrm{V}_1=\mathrm{V}_2=0$ (for statement-1) $\therefore \mathrm{P}_1-\mathrm{P}_2=\mathrm{g}\left(\mathrm{h}_2-\mathrm{h}_2\right)$
For statement-2 $\mathrm{P}_1+\frac{1}{2} \rho \mathrm{v}_1^2=\mathrm{P}_2+\frac{1}{2} \rho \mathrm{v}_2^2$ $\begin{aligned} & P_1-P_2=\rho g h \\ & P_1-P_2=\frac{1}{2} \rho v_2^2-\frac{1}{2} \rho v_1^2 \\ & \rho g h=\frac{1}{2} \rho v_2^2-\frac{1}{2} \rho v_1^2 \\ & 2 g h=v_2^2-v_1^2 \end{aligned}$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: Three resistors of $4 \Omega, 6 \Omega$ and $12 \Omega$ are connected in parallel and the combination is connected in series with a $1.5 \mathrm{~V}$ battery of $1 \Omega$ internal resistance. The rate of Joule heating in the $4 \Omega$ resistor is
questionIds.solution:
Resistors $4 \Omega, 6 \Omega$ and $12 \Omega$ are connected in parallel, its equivalent resistance $(R)$ is given by

$$
\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{12} \Rightarrow R=\frac{12}{6}=2 \Omega
$$
Again $R$ is connected to $1.5 \mathrm{~V}$ battery whose internal resistance $r=1 \Omega$.
Equivalent resistance now,
$$
R^{\prime}=2 \Omega+1 \Omega=3 \Omega
$$
Current, $I_{\text {total }}=\frac{V}{R^{\prime}}=\frac{1.5}{3}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~A}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& I_{\text {total }}=\frac{1}{2}=3 x+2 x+x=6 x \\
& \Rightarrow x=\frac{1}{12}
\end{aligned}
$$
$\therefore$ Current through $4 \Omega$ resistor $=3 x$
$$
=3 \times \frac{1}{12}=\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{~A}
$$
Therefore, rate of Joule heating in the $4 \Omega$ resistor
$$
=I^2 R=\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^2 \times 4=\frac{1}{4}=0.25 \mathrm{~W}
$$

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: Freezing point of an aqueous solution is $(-0.186)^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Elevation of boiling point of the same solution is $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}=0.512^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}=1.86^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, find the increase in boiling point.
questionIds.solution: $\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}} \times \frac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}} \times \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{A}}} \times 1000 ; \Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \frac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}} \times \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{A}}} \times 1000 ; \frac{\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}}{\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}}=\frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}}{-0.186}=\frac{0.512}{1.86}=0.0512^0 \mathrm{C}$

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: According to Lewis theory, the total number of $\sigma$ bond-pairs and lone pair of electrons around the central atom of $XeO_6^{4-}$ ion is _____.
questionIds.solution:
The central atom in $XeO_6^{4-}$ is Xenon (Xe).

The number of valence electrons in Xe is $8$.

Let the oxidation state of Xe in $XeO_6^{4-}$ be $x$.

$x + 6(-2) = -4$

$x = +8$

Since the oxidation state of Xe is $+8$, all $8$ of its valence electrons are involved in bonding with the $6$ oxygen atoms. There are no non-bonding electrons left on the central atom.

Number of lone pairs on Xe $= 0$

Xe forms $6$ $\sigma$ bonds with the $6$ oxygen atoms (and $2$ $\pi$ bonds to satisfy its $+8$ oxidation state and minimize formal charge).

Number of $\sigma$ bond-pairs $= 6$

Total number of $\sigma$ bond-pairs and lone pairs around the central atom $= 6 + 0 = 6$.

Answer: $6$

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>KCl</mi></math> solution of conductivity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>14</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> shows a resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>19</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> in a conductivity cell. If the same cell is filled with an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HCl</mi></math> solution, the resistance drops to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>03</mn><mi>Ω</mi></math>. The conductivity of the HCl solution is ____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. (Round off to the Nearest Integer).
questionIds.solution:
The thermal conductivity of a material is a measure of its ability to conduct heat. It is commonly denoted by κ, \lambda, or \kappa. Heat transfer occurs at a lower rate in materials of low thermal conductivity than in materials of high thermal conductivity

κ=1R·G*

For same conductivity cell, G* is constant and hence κ·R.= constant.

∴0.14×4.19=κ×1.03

or, =0.14×4.191.03

=0.5695Sm-1

=56.95×10-2Sm-1≈57×10-2Sm-1

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q:
At the sea level, the dry air mass percentage composition is given as nitrogen gas : 70.0, oxygen gas : 27.0 and argon gas : 3.0. If total pressure is 1.15 atm , then calculate the ratio of followings respectively :
(i) partial pressure of nitrogen gas to partial pressure of oxygen gas
(ii) partial pressure of oxygen gas to partial pressure of argon gas
(Given : Molar mass of $\mathrm{N}, \mathrm{O}$ and Ar are 14, 16, and $40 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively)
questionIds.solution:
$\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{N}_2}}{\mathrm{Po}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{N}_2} \cdot \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{T}}}{\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{O}_2} \cdot \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{T}}}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{N}_2}}{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{O}_2}}$ {using Dalton's law of partial pressure}
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{70 / 28}{27 / 32}=2.96 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_2}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{Ar}}}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{O}_2}}{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{Ar}}}=\frac{27 / 32}{3 / 40}=11.25\end{aligned}$

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q:
Thyroxine, the hormone has given below structure

The percentage of iodine in thyroxine is ____ $\%$. (nearest integer)
(Given molar mass in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{C}: 12. \mathrm{H}: 1, \mathrm{O}: 16$, $\mathrm{N}: 14, \mathrm{I}: 127)$
questionIds.solution:
$\rightarrow$ Molecular formula of Thyroxine $\Rightarrow$ $\mathrm{C}_{15} \mathrm{H}_{11} \mathrm{O}_4 \mathrm{NI}_4$
$\rightarrow$ Molecular mass of Thyroxine -
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{C} \rightarrow 15 \times 12=180 \\ & \mathrm{H} \rightarrow 11 \times 1=11 \\ & \mathrm{O} \rightarrow 16 \times 4=64 \\ & \mathrm{~N} \rightarrow 14 \times 1=14 \\ & \mathrm{I} \rightarrow 127 \times 4=508\end{aligned}$
$\rightarrow$ Molecular mass of Thyroxine $\Rightarrow 777$
$\begin{aligned}& \rightarrow \% \text { of Iodine }=\frac{508}{777} \times 100 \\& =65.38 \%\end{aligned}$
Nearest integer $=65$

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q:
Consider the following reduction processes :
$\mathrm{Al}^{3+}+3 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{s}), \mathrm{E}^{0}=-1.66 \mathrm{~V}$
$\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}, \mathrm{E}^{0}=+0.77 \mathrm{~V}$
$\mathrm{Co}^{3+}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Co}^{2+}, \mathrm{E}^{0}=+1.81 \mathrm{~V}$
$\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+3 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cr}(\mathrm{s}), \mathrm{E}^{\circ}=-0.74 \mathrm{~V}$
The tendency to act as reducing agent decreases in the order :
questionIds.solution:
Reducing agent strength is determined by oxidation potential (opposite sign of reduction potential). Stronger reducing agents have more negative oxidation potentials (easier to oxidize).
Oxidation potentials:
Al(s) → Al³⁺ has E° = +1.66 V (strongest reducing agent);
Cr(s) → Cr³⁺ has E° = +0.74 V;
Fe²⁺ → Fe³⁺ has E° = -0.77 V;
Co²⁺ → Co³⁺ has E° = -1.81 V (weakest reducing agent).
Order of decreasing reducing agent tendency: Al > Cr > Fe²⁺ > Co²⁺.

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: A species having carbon with sextet of electrons and can act as electrophile is called
questionIds.solution:
A carbocation is a molecule in which a carbon atom has a positive charge and three bonds. We can say that, basically, they are carbon cations. Formerly, it was known as carbonium ion. Carbocation today is defined as any even-electron cation that possesses a significant positive charge on the carbon atom.

The carbon cations are very reactive and unstable due to an incomplete octet. In simple words, carbocations do not have eight electrons; therefore, they do not satisfy the octet rule.

In carbocation, the hybridisation of carbon will be sp2 and its shape is trigonal planar. There is also a vacant p- orbital which indicates its electron-deficient nature. The carbon has 6 electrons in its valence shell. Due to this, it is an electron-deficient species, also known as an electrophile.

Six electron species

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: Which one of the following arrangements represents the correct order of the proton affinity of the given species :
questionIds.solution:
The species with the greatest proton affinity will be the strongest base, and its conjugate acid will be the weakest acid. The weakest acid will have the smallest value of $\mathrm{K}_a$. Since $\mathrm{HI}$ is a stronger acid than $\mathrm{HF}$ which is a stronger acid than $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{S}$, a partial order of proton affinity is 
$\mathrm{I}^{-} < \mathrm{F}^{-} < \mathrm{HS}^{-}$
Since $\mathrm{NH}_3$ is a very weak acid, $\mathrm{NH}_2^{-}$must be a very strong base. Therefore the correct order of proton affinity is
$\mathrm{I}^{-} < \mathrm{F}^{-} < \mathrm{HS}^{-} < \mathrm{NH}_2^{-}$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q:
Let μ  be the mean and σ be the standard deviation of the distribution

Xi 0 1 2 3 4 5
fi k+2 2k k2-1 k2-1 k2+1 k-3

where Σfi=62. If x denotes the greatest integer ≤x, thenμ2 + σ2  is equal to
questionIds.solution:
Given,

Xi 0 1 2 3 4 5
fi k+2 2k k2-1 k2-1 k2+1 k-3

Now we know that,

Mean=∑Xifi∑fi

⇒μ=2k+ 2k2 –2 + 3k2–3 + 4k2+4 +5k – 1562

⇒μ=9k2+7k-1662 .......1

And ∑fi=62

⇒3k2+4k-2=62

⇒3k2+4k-64=0

⇒(3k+16)(k-4)=0

⇒k=4

Now putting the value of k in above equation 1 we get,

μ=15662

Now finding variance we get,

σ2=∑Xi2fi∑fi-Mean2

⇒σ2=1·2k+4k2-1+9k2-1+16k2+1+25k-362-156622

⇒σ2=29k2+27k-7262-μ2

Now putting the value of k in above equation we get,

⇒σ2=50062-μ2

⇒σ2+μ2=50062

⇒σ2+μ2=8

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If $\left(2+\frac{x}{3}\right)^{55}$ is expanded in the ascending powers of $x$ and the coefficients of powers of $x$ in two consecutive terms of the expansion are equal, then these terms are:
questionIds.solution:
Let $r^{\text {th }}$ and $(r+1)^{\text {th }}$ term has equal coefficient
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\left(2+\frac{x}{3}\right)^{55}=2^{55}\left(1+\frac{x}{6}\right)^{55} \\
&r^{\text {th }} \text { term }=2^{5555} C_r\left(\frac{x}{6}\right)^r
\end{aligned}
$$
Coefficient of $x^r$ is $2^{55}{ }^{55} C_r \frac{1}{6^r}$
$$
(r+1)^{\text {th }} \text { term }=2^{5555} C_{r+1}\left(\frac{x}{6}\right)^{r+1}
$$
Coefficient of $x^{r+1}$ is $2^{55}{ }^{55} C_{r+1} \cdot \frac{1}{6^{r+1}}$
Both coefficients are equal
$$
2^{5555} C_r \frac{1}{6^r}=2^{5555} C_{r+1} \frac{1}{6^{r+1}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\frac{1}{|r| 55-r}=\frac{1}{\lfloor r+1 \mid 54-r} \cdot \frac{1}{6} \\
&6(r+1)=55-r \\
&6 r+6=55-r \\
&7 r=49 \\
&r=7 \\
&(r+1)=8
\end{aligned}
$$
Coefficient of $7^{\text {th }}$ and $8^{\text {th }}$ terms are equal.

A function is surjective, when the range of the function is same as the co-domain.

Now, y=x21-x2

⇒y-yx2=x2 

⇒y=x2y+1

⇒x2=yy+1

∵  x2≥0 ⇒yy+1≥0

⇒y ∈-∞, -1∪0, ∞ or R-−1, 0.

∴ Range of f=A=R-−1, 0.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: A square of side a lies above the $x$-axis and has one vertex at the origin. The side passing through the origin makes an angle $\alpha\left(0 < \alpha < \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$ with the positive direction of $\mathrm{x}$-axis. The equation of its diagonal not passing through the origin is
questionIds.solution:
Co-ordinates of $\mathrm{A}=(a \cos \alpha, a \sin \alpha)$ Equation of $\mathrm{OB}, \mathrm{y}=\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\alpha\right) \mathrm{x}$
$\mathrm{CA} \perp \mathrm{r}$ to OB $\quad \therefore$ slope of $\mathrm{CA}=-\cot \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+2\right)$ Equation of $\mathrm{CA} \mathrm{y}-\mathrm{a} \sin \alpha=-\cot \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+2\right)(\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{a} \cos \alpha)$ $\mathrm{y}(\sin \alpha+\cos \alpha)+\mathrm{x}(\cos \alpha-\sin \alpha)=\mathrm{a}$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>…</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>x</mi><mi>n</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msub><mi>h</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msub><mi>h</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>…</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msub><mi>h</mi><mi>n</mi></msub></mfrac></math> are two A.P.s such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>h</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mo>&</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>8</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>h</mi><mn>7</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>5</mn></msub><mo>·</mo><msub><mi>h</mi><mn>10</mn></msub></math> is equal to
questionIds.solution:
Let d1 be the common difference of the A.P. x1,x2,…, then x8-x3=5 d1=12⇒

⇒x5=x3+2 d1=8+2×125=12.8

Let d2 be the common difference of the other sequence then

5 d2=120-18=-340⇒d2

⇒1 h10=1 h7+3 d2=1200⇒h10=200

⇒x5·h10=12.8×200=2560

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If the solution $y(x)$ of the given differential equation $\left(\mathrm{e}^y+1\right) \cos x \mathrm{~d} x+\mathrm{e}^y \sin x \mathrm{~d} y=0$ passes through the point $\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, 0\right)$, then the value of $\mathrm{e}^{y\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)}$ is equal to_________
questionIds.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \left(\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}+1\right) \cos \mathrm{x} \mathrm{dx}+\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}} \sin \mathrm{x} d \mathrm{y}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{d}\left(\left(\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}+1\right) \sin \mathrm{x}\right)=0 \\ & \left(\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}+1\right) \sin \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{C} \end{aligned}$
It passes through $\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, 0\right)$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=2$ Now, $x=\frac{\pi}{6}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}=3$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The area of the region bounded by the curves $y=|x-1|$ and $y=3-|x|$ is
questionIds.solution: $A=\int_{-1}^0\{(3+x)-(-x+1)\} d x+\int_0^1\{(3-x)-(-x+1)\} d x+\int_1^2\{(3-x)-(-x-1)\} d x$ $=\int_{-1}^0(2+2 x) d x+\int_0^1 2 d x+\int_1^2(4-2 x) d x$ $=\left[2 x-x^2\right]_{-1}^0+[2 x]_0^1+\left[4 x-x^2\right]_1^2$ $=0-(-2+1)+(2-0)+(8-4)-(4-1)$ $=1+2+4-3=4$ sq. units

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If for <math><mi>x</mi><mo>≥</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>y</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is the solution of the differential equation, <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>d</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msup><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>y</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> then <math><mi>y</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to ________
questionIds.solution:
dydx=1+x+y-31+x

dydx-11+xy=1+x-3(1+x)

I.F=e-∫1(1+x)dx=1(1+x)

∴ddxy1+x=x+31+x-1+c

⇒y1+x=x+31+x-1+c

⇒y=1+xx+3(1+x)+c , ∴ at x=2, y=0

⇒0=32+1+c ⇒c=-3

At x=3, y=3

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The area (in sq. units) of the region enclosed between the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></math> and the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> is ______.
questionIds.solution:
y2=2x ⇒y2=24-y

⇒y2+2y-8=0  ⇒y=-4,2

The point of intersection of y2=2x and x+y=4 are 8,-4 and 2,2

Area =∫-424-y-y22dy =4y-y22-y36-42

=8-2-43--16-8+323

=543=18 Sq.units.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let the system of linear equations <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mi>α</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mi>α</mi></math> be inconsistent. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> is equal to
questionIds.solution:
Given, the system of linear equations x+2y+z=2, αx+3y-z=α, and -αx+y+2z=-α 

Now for system to be inconsistent, ∆=0 & either of  ∆1,∆2 or ∆3 ≠0

So, Δ=12123-1-212

=6+y-22α-α+1α+3α

=7-2α+4α

=7+2α

Δ=0⇒α=-72

Now calculating, Δ1=221α3-1-α12

=14+2α

Where α=-72 so, ∆1=14-7=7

Δ1≠0, hence system is inconsistent at α=-72

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><munder><mi>lim</mi><mrow><mi>n</mi><mo>→</mo><mo>∞</mo></mrow></munder><mfrac><mrow><mfenced close="]" open="["><mi>r</mi></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>+</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>n</mi><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow><msup><mi>n</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is non-zero real number and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mi>r</mi></mfenced></math> denotes the greatest integer less than or equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi><mo>,</mo></math> is equal to :
questionIds.solution:
We know that r≤r<r+1

2r≤2r<2r+1

3r≤3r<3r+1

⋮  ⋮   ⋮

nr≤nr<nr+1

On adding all the above inequalities, we get

r+2r+…+nr≤r+2r+...+nr<r+2r+...nr+n

Using 1+2+3+...+n=nn+12, n∈N,

nn+12·r≤r+2r+...+nr<nn+12·r+n

⇒nn+12·rn2≤r+2r+...+nrn2<nn+12·r+nn2

Now, limn→∞n(n+1)·r2·n2=limn→∞n21+1n·r2·n2=r2

and limn→∞nn+12·r+nn2=limn→∞n21+1n·r+1n2·n2=r2

So, by Sandwich Theorem, we can conclude that

limn→∞r+2r+...+nrn2=r2.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: In an examination, there are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> multiple choice questions with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> choices, out of which exactly one is correct. There are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> marks for each correct answer, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></math> marks for each wrong answer and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn></math> mark if the question is not attempted. Then, the number of ways a student appearing in the examination gets <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> marks is _____
questionIds.solution:
Let the marks obtained in each of the five MCQ be x1, x2, x3, x4, x5

Now, x1+x2+x3+x4+x5=5

Only one possibility satisfies the above equation i.e.  3,3,3,-2,-2

Hence, total number of ways =5!3!2!×2×2=40

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The number of values of $z \in \mathbb{C}$, satisfying the equations $|z-(4+8i)|=\sqrt{10}$ and $|z-(3+5i)|+|z-(5+11i)|=4\sqrt{5}$, is:
questionIds.solution:
The first equation $|z-(4+8i)|=\sqrt{10}$ represents a circle with center $C(4, 8)$ and radius $r = \sqrt{10}$.

The second equation $|z-(3+5i)|+|z-(5+11i)|=4\sqrt{5}$ represents an ellipse with foci at $S_1(3, 5)$ and $S_2(5, 11)$, and length of the major axis $2a = 4\sqrt{5}$.

The distance between the foci is $2ae = \sqrt{(5-3)^2 + (11-5)^2} = \sqrt{4+36} = \sqrt{40} = 2\sqrt{10}$.

The center of the ellipse is the midpoint of the line segment joining the foci, which is $\left(\dfrac{3+5}{2}, \dfrac{5+11}{2}\right) = (4, 8)$. This is the same as the center of the circle.

The semi-major axis is $a = 2\sqrt{5}$, so $a^2 = 20$.

The semi-minor axis $b$ is given by $b^2 = a^2 - (ae)^2 = 20 - 10 = 10$, which gives $b = \sqrt{10}$.

Since the circle and the ellipse are concentric and the radius of the circle $r = \sqrt{10}$ is equal to the semi-minor axis $b$ of the ellipse, the circle is inscribed in the ellipse and touches it exactly at the two endpoints of the minor axis.

Therefore, there are exactly $2$ points of intersection, meaning there are $2$ values of $z$ satisfying both equations.

Answer: $2$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q:
If x=a, y=b, z=c is a solution of the system of linear equations
x+8y+7z=0
9x+2y+3z=0
x+y+z=0
Such that the point a,b,c  lies on the plane x+2y+z=6 , then 2a+b+c equals:
questionIds.solution:
x+8y+7z=0  ...i        9x+2y+3z=0  ...ii        x+y+z=0  ...iii             .....

Solve the equation i &iii

 ⇒7y+6z=0

Solve the equation ii &iii

⇒7x+z=0

x=λ & y=-6-7λ7 & z=-7λ

x=λ& y=6λ& z=-7λ

  ⇒λ+12λ−7λ=6⇒6λ=6⇒λ=1 | ⇒2λ+6λ−7λ⇒λ=1

Hence, a=1, b=6, c=-7

∴2a+b+c=1

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let $\mathrm{S}=\frac{1}{25!}+\frac{1}{3!23!}+\frac{1}{5!21!}+\ldots$ up to 13 terms. If $13 \mathrm{~S}=\frac{2^{k}}{n!}, k \in \mathrm{~N}$, then $n+k$ is equal to
questionIds.solution:
The general term is $\frac{1}{(2r-1)!(27-2r)!}$ for $r=1$ to $13$.
Multiply and divide by $26!$: $\frac{1}{(2r-1)!(27-2r)!} = \frac{1}{26!}\binom{26}{2r-1}$
$S = \frac{1}{26!}\sum_{r=1}^{13}\binom{26}{2r-1} = \frac{1}{26!}\left[\binom{26}{1}+\binom{26}{3}+\cdots+\binom{26}{25}\right]$
Sum of odd-indexed binomial coefficients: $\sum \binom{26}{\text{odd}} = 2^{25}$
$S = \frac{2^{25}}{26!}$
$13S = \frac{13 \cdot 2^{25}}{26!} = \frac{13 \cdot 2^{25}}{26 \cdot 25!} = \frac{2^{25}}{2 \cdot 25!} = \frac{2^{24}}{25!}$
So $k = 24,\; n = 25$, giving $n + k = 49$.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The domain of the function $f(x)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{|x|-x}}$ is
questionIds.solution:
$\frac{1}{\sqrt{|x|-x}} \Rightarrow|x|-x>0 \Rightarrow|x|>x \Rightarrow x$ is negative
$x \in(-\infty, 0)$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: In a survey of 220 students of a higher secondary school, it was found that at least 125 and at most 130 students studied Mathematics; at least 85 and at most 95 studied Physics; at least 75 and at most 90 studied Chemistry; 30 studied both Physics and Chemistry; 50 studied both Chemistry and Mathematics; 40 studied both Mathematics and Physics and 10 studied none of these subjects. Let $\mathrm{m}$ and $\mathrm{n}$ respectively be the least and the most number of students who studied all the three subjects. Then $\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}$ is equal to ______
questionIds.solution: $\begin{aligned} & 125 \leq \mathrm{m}+90-\mathrm{x} \leq 130 \\ & 85 \leq \mathrm{P}+70-\mathrm{x} \leq 95 \\ & 75 \leq \mathrm{C}+80-\mathrm{x} \leq 90 \\ & \mathrm{~m}+\mathrm{P}+\mathrm{C}+120-2 \mathrm{x}=210 \\ & \Rightarrow 15 \leq \mathrm{x} \leq 45 \& 30-\mathrm{x} \geq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow 15 \leq \mathrm{x} \leq 30 \\ & 30+15=45\end{aligned}$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The solution curve, of the differential equation $2 y \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+3=5 \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$, passing through the point $(0,1)$ is a conic, whose vertex lies on the line:
questionIds.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & (2 y-5) \frac{d y}{d x}=-3 \\ & (2 y-5) d y=-3 d x \\ & 2 \cdot \frac{y^2}{2}-5 y=-3 x+\lambda\end{aligned}$
$\because$ Curve passes through $(0,1)$
$\Rightarrow \lambda=-4$
$\because$ Curve will be
$\left(y-\frac{5}{2}\right)^2=-3\left(x-\frac{3}{4}\right)$
$\therefore$ Vertex of parabola will be $\left(\frac{3}{4}, \frac{5}{2}\right)$
$\because 2 x+3 y=9$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If a→ and b→ are unit vectors, then the greatest value of 3|a→+b→|+|a→-b→| is
questionIds.solution:
Let angle between a→ and b→=α.
So, 3|a→+b→|+|a→-b→|=32+2cosα+2-2cosα
=32×2cos2α2+2×2sin2α2
=23cosα2+sinα2

As α∈0,π⇒α2∈0,π2

So, given expression is

=23cosα2+sinα2
Thus, maximum value is=232+12=2×2=4

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let $\vec{a}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \vec{b}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}$ and $\vec{c}=\vec{a} \times \vec{b}$. Let $\vec{d}$ be a vector such that $|\vec{d}-\vec{a}|=\sqrt{11},|\vec{c} \times \vec{d}|=3$ and the angle between $\vec{c}$ and $\vec{d}$ is $\frac{\pi}{4}$. Then $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{d}$ is equal to
questionIds.solution:
$\vec{c} = \vec{a} \times \vec{b} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & 1 & -2 \\ 1 & 1 & 0 \end{vmatrix} = 2\hat{i} - 2\hat{j} + \hat{k}$
$|\vec{c}| = \sqrt{4+4+1} = 3$, $|\vec{a}| = \sqrt{4+1+4} = 3$
From $|\vec{c} \times \vec{d}| = 3$ and $\cos(\angle(\vec{c}, \vec{d})) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$:
$|\vec{c}||\vec{d}|\sin\frac{\pi}{4} = 3$ → $3|\vec{d}| \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = 3$ → $|\vec{d}| = \sqrt{2}$
From $|\vec{d} - \vec{a}|^2 = 11$:
$|\vec{d}|^2 - 2\vec{a} \cdot \vec{d} + |\vec{a}|^2 = 11$
$2 - 2\vec{a} \cdot \vec{d} + 9 = 11$
$\vec{a} \cdot \vec{d} = 0$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q:
Let λ∈R. The system of linear equations
2x1-4x2+λx3=1
x1-6x2+x3=2
λx1-10x2+4x3=3
is inconsistent for :
questionIds.solution:
Here, we will use Cramer's Rule according to which, D=0 and, D1≠0 if the system of linear equations is inconsistent. D=2-4λ1-61λ-104=2-24+10+44-λ+λ-10+6λ

=23λ2-7λ-6=2λ-33λ+2

D=0⇒λ=3,-23

D1=1-4λ2-613-104=1-24+10+48-3+λ-20+18=23-λ

For   λ=-23,D1≠0

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The area of the region, inside the ellipse $x^{2}+4 y^{2}=4$ and outside the region bounded by the curves $y=|x|-1$ and $y=1-|x|$, is :
questionIds.solution:
Ellipse: $\frac{x^2}{4} + y^2 = 1$ with $a = 2$, $b = 1$. Area $= \pi ab = 2\pi$.
Curves $y = |x| - 1$ and $y = 1 - |x|$ intersect at $x = \pm 1$, forming a rhombus with vertices $(0, 1)$, $(1, 0)$, $(0, -1)$, $(-1, 0)$.
Vertices $(0, \pm 1)$ lie on ellipse; $(\pm 1, 0)$ are inside ellipse.
Area of rhombus $= \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 2 = 2$.
Required area $= 2\pi - 2 = 2(\pi - 1)$.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The general solution of the differential equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>5</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mi>d</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is
questionIds.solution:
Given, x-y2dx+y5x+y2dy=0

After rearranging we get,

dydx=y2-xy5x+y2 

Now, let y2=v⇒2ydydx=dvdx

12ydvdx=v-xy5x+v

dvdx=2v-x5x+v

Now let v=kx

⇒k+xdkdx=2kx-x5x+kx

⇒xdkdx=-k2+3k+2k+5

⇒∫5+kk+1k+2dk=∫-dxx

⇒∫4k+1-3k+2dk=-∫dxx

⇒4lnk+1-3lnk+2=-lnx+lnc

⇒k+14k+23=cx

⇒vx+14vx+23=cx

⇒cy2+2x3=y2+x4

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> be a plane containing the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>z</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> and parallel to the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>z</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow><mn>7</mn></mfrac></math>. If the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>)</mo></math> lies on the plane <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mrow><mn>5</mn><mi>α</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to ___ .
questionIds.solution:
Equation of plane is x-1y+6z+53424-37=0

Now (1,-1,α)  lies on it so

05α+53424-37=0⇒5α+38=0⇒5α=38

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The values of ' $a$ ' for which one root of the equation $x^2-(a+1) x+a^2+a-8=0$ exceeds 2 and the other is lesser than 2 , are given by :
questionIds.solution:
$$
x^2-(a+1) x+a^2+a-8=0
$$
Since roots are different, therefore $\mathrm{D}>0$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow(a+1)^2-4\left(a^2+a-8\right)>0 \\
& \Rightarrow(a-3)(3 a+1) < 0
\end{aligned}
$$
There are two cases arises.
Case I. $a-3>0$ and $3 a+1 < 0$
$\Rightarrow a>3$ and $a < -\frac{11}{3}$
Hence, no solution in this case
Case II : $a-3 < 0$ and $3 a+11>0$
$\Rightarrow a < 3$ and $a>-\frac{11}{3}$
$\therefore \quad-\frac{11}{3} < a < 3 \Rightarrow-2 < a < 3$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let f be a real valued function, defined onR--1,1 and given by fx=3logex-1x+1-2x-1. Then in which of the following intervals, function fx is increasing?
questionIds.solution:
fx=3logex-1-3logex+1-2x-1

f'x=3x-1-3x+1+2x-12

f'x=42x-1x-12x+1

f'x≥0

⇒  x∈-∞,-1∪12,1∪1,∞ or -∞,-1∪12,∞-1

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If <math><mi>g</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>g</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>f</mi></mrow></mfenced><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>10</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mi></mi></math> then <math><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mn>5</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
questionIds.solution:
gfx=4542-10.54+5=-54
gf54=f254+f54-1
-54=f254+f54-1
f254+f54+14=0
f54+122=0
f54=-12

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: $\lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} \frac{\tan \left(5(x)^{\frac{1}{3}}\right) \log _e\left(1+3 x^2\right)}{\left(\tan ^{-1} 3 \sqrt{x}\right)^2\left(e^{5(x)^{\frac{4}{3}}}-1\right)}$ is equal to
questionIds.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{\cdot}}\left(\frac{\tan \left(5 \mathrm{x}^{1 / 3}\right)}{5 \mathrm{x}^{1 / 3}}\right) \cdot\left(\frac{(3 \sqrt{\mathrm{x}})^2}{\left(\tan ^{-1} 3 \sqrt{\mathrm{x}}\right)^2}\right)\left(\frac{\ell\left(1+3 \mathrm{x}^2\right)}{3 \mathrm{x}^2}\right)\left(\frac{5 \mathrm{x}^{4 / 3}}{\mathrm{e}^{5 x^4}-1}\right) \times \frac{5 \mathrm{x}^{1 / 3} \cdot 3 \mathrm{x}^2}{5 \mathrm{x}^{4 / 3} \cdot 9 \mathrm{x}} \\ & =\frac{1}{3}\end{aligned}$
